syllabus_desc: Explain the basic characteristics of an electrical circuit.
concept: An electrical circuit's basically a complete path where current flows from the power source, through the load (your equipment), and back. You've got three key bits: the source (like your battery), the conductors (wiring), and the load (whatever uses the power). Current only flows when you've got a complete path—break it anywhere and nothing happens. Think of it like water flowing through pipes; if you block it, flow stops.
exam_tip: Remember: circuit must be complete for current to flow. Examiners love asking what happens when you open a switch.
key_facts: Complete conducting path required
Current flows from positive to negative terminal
Open circuit = no current flow
Series vs parallel affects total resistance
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: State Ohm’s law.
concept: Ohm's law is the foundation of everything electrical in your aircraft: Voltage (V) equals Current (I) times Resistance (R). Written as V = IR. Think of it like water flowing through a pipe—voltage is the pressure pushing the water, current is the flow rate, and resistance is how narrow the pipe is. If you increase the voltage (more pressure), you get more current (more flow). If you increase the resistance (narrower pipe), you get less current for the same voltage. Rearrange it three ways: V = IR, I = V/R, R = V/I. You'll use this constantly when thinking about why a circuit behaves the way it does. In your helicopter's 28-volt system, if a landing light has 14 ohms resistance, it draws 2 amps (28/14 = 2). Simple maths, but it underpins every electrical calculation you'll ever do.
exam_tip: Know all three rearrangements: V = IR, I = V/R, R = V/I. They'll give you a scenario with two values and ask for the third. Units: volts, amps, ohms.
key_facts: Ohm's law: V = IR (Voltage = Current × Resistance). Three forms: V = IR, I = V/R, R = V/I. Voltage measured in volts (V). Current measured in amperes (A). Resistance measured in ohms (Ω). Higher voltage = more current (for same resistance). Higher resistance = less current (for same voltage).
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Describe the terms: (a) electromotive force (volts); (b) current (amperes); (c) resistance (ohms).
concept: Electromotive force, or EMF, is the voltage your source provides—like your aircraft battery pushing 28 volts. Current's the actual flow of electrons through your circuit, measured in amperes; think of it as how much 'stuff' is moving. Resistance is opposition to that flow, measured in ohms—thicker wires have less resistance, thin ones have more. Your engine starter draws massive current because it's low resistance; your navigation lights draw far less.
exam_tip: EMF is the source voltage, current is the flow, resistance opposes it. You'll get scenarios asking which increases and which decreases with circuit changes.
key_facts: EMF = voltage provided by source (volts)
Current = flow of electrons (amperes)
Resistance = opposition to flow (ohms)
EMF stays constant regardless of load
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Describe the structure of a: (a) two-wire circuit; (b) grounded ‘earth return’ electrical circuit.
concept: A two-wire circuit is the straightforward approach: one wire carries current from the battery to the component (positive), another carries it back (negative). Each component has two wires running to it. Simple, reliable, but heavy—all that copper adds up in an aircraft where every gram matters. That's where the earth return system comes in. Instead of running a second wire back, you use the aircraft's metal structure as the return path. One wire goes from the battery to the component, and the return current flows through the airframe back to the battery. Most light helicopters like the R22 use earth return—it saves roughly 40% of the wiring weight. The downside? If the airframe connection corrodes or works loose, the circuit fails. That's why you'll find bonding straps and clean attachment points throughout the airframe. Composite structures can't conduct electricity, so composite helicopters may need more two-wire circuits or bonding conductors.
exam_tip: Two-wire = separate positive and negative wires. Earth return = airframe is the negative return path. Know the weight advantage and the corrosion risk of earth return systems.
key_facts: Two-wire: separate positive and negative conductors to each component. Earth return: airframe structure serves as negative return path. Earth return saves ~40% wiring weight. Earth return requires good bonding and clean connections. Corrosion at connection points can cause circuit failure. Composite structures cannot serve as earth return without bonding conductors.
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Describe the structure of a: — (b) grounded ‘earth return’ electrical circuit

syllabus_desc: Distinguish between direct and alternating current.
concept: Direct current, or DC, flows one way only—from positive to negative—which is what your battery provides. Alternating current, or AC, constantly reverses direction, changing polarity back and forth. Your aircraft runs on DC from the battery and alternator, but some older systems or test equipment might produce AC. AC's actually generated more easily in alternators, then we convert it to DC with a rectifier because that's what the aircraft electrical system needs.
exam_tip: DC = one direction always (battery/alternator output). AC = constantly reversing (found in alternators before rectification). Know why aircraft prefer DC.
key_facts: DC = unidirectional flow (positive to negative)
AC = bidirectional, constantly reversing
Aircraft electrical systems are DC-based
Alternators generate AC, then it's rectified to DC
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the terms: (a) frequency (Hertz); (b) rectification.
concept: Frequency's how many times per second AC reverses direction, measured in Hertz—your aircraft alternator typically produces around 400 Hz. Rectification's the process of converting that AC to DC, done by a rectifier inside the alternator. Think of it like a one-way valve; it lets current flow one direction but blocks the reverse, so out comes DC current you can actually use for charging the battery and running systems.
exam_tip: Frequency = cycles per second (Hz). Rectification = converting AC to DC using a rectifier. You might get asked about typical aircraft alternator frequency.
key_facts: Frequency measured in Hertz (cycles per second)
Aircraft alternators produce ~400 Hz
Rectification = AC to DC conversion
Rectifier acts as electronic one-way valve
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the terms: — (a) frequency (Hertz)
key_facts: [PENDING]

syllabus_desc: Explain the terms: — (b) rectification

syllabus_desc: Explain the properties of magnetism, including: (a) polarity; (b) attraction; (c) repulsion.
concept: Every magnet's got a north and south pole—that's polarity. Opposite poles attract each other (north pulls south), same poles repel (north repels north). This is the foundation of how your alternator, starter motor, and relays work. The magnetic field around a magnet is strongest at the poles and weakest at the centre. You'll see this in action with electromagnetic switches and the magneto's ignition system.
exam_tip: Opposite poles attract, same poles repel. Remember this when explaining how electromagnets create switching action in relays.
key_facts: Polarity: north and south poles
Opposite poles attract
Same poles repel
Magnetic field strongest at poles
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the properties of magnetism, including: — (a) polarity
key_facts: [PENDING]

syllabus_desc: Explain the properties of magnetism, including: — (b) attraction

syllabus_desc: Distinguish between: (a) temporary and permanent magnets; Advisory Circular AC61-5 Revision 38 Sub Topic Syllabus Item (b) the properties of ‘soft iron’ and ‘hard iron’ magnets.
concept: Temporary magnets only hold their magnetism while they're being magnetised—remove the magnetising force and they lose it. Permanent magnets retain their magnetism indefinitely (well, a very long time). This matters in your aircraft because different components need different magnetic behaviour. Soft iron magnetises easily but loses it quickly—it's a temporary magnet. That makes it perfect for electromagnets, relay cores, and transformer cores where you want the magnetism to switch on and off with the current. Hard iron (or hard steel) is difficult to magnetise initially, but once magnetised it holds its field stubbornly—that's your permanent magnet material. You'll find it in magnetos, compass needles, and permanent magnet generators. The compass in your helicopter uses a permanent magnet to align with the Earth's field. The starter solenoid uses a soft iron core that only becomes magnetic when you press the starter button. Each material is chosen for what the job demands.
exam_tip: Soft iron = easy to magnetise, easy to demagnetise = temporary magnets = electromagnets, relays, transformers. Hard iron = hard to magnetise, retains field = permanent magnets = magnetos, compass needles. They'll ask which type suits which application.
key_facts: Temporary magnets: magnetised only while force applied. Permanent magnets: retain magnetism indefinitely. Soft iron: easily magnetised and demagnetised (temporary). Hard iron/steel: difficult to magnetise, retains field (permanent). Soft iron used in: electromagnets, relay cores, transformer cores. Hard iron used in: magnetos, compass needles, permanent magnet generators.
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Distinguish between: — (a) temporary and permanent magnets

syllabus_desc: Describe the terms: (a) magnetic field; (b) magnetic flux; (c) permeability.
concept: The magnetic field is the region around a magnet where magnetic force acts—you can visualise it with field lines. Magnetic flux is the total amount of magnetic field, the sum of all those lines of force flowing through a given area. Permeability's how easily a material lets magnetic field pass through it—soft iron's highly permeable, air isn't. In your alternator, the iron core has high permeability, so the magnetic field concentrates efficiently there rather than spreading into the air.
exam_tip: Field = region of force. Flux = total field strength through an area. Permeability = how easily material lets field pass through. You'll explain how alternator cores use high permeability materials.
key_facts: Magnetic field = region of magnetic force
Magnetic flux = total field through area
Permeability = ease of magnetic field passage
Soft iron has high permeability
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Describe the terms: — (a) magnetic field

syllabus_desc: Explain electromagnetism.
concept: When current flows through a conductor, it creates a magnetic field around it—that's electromagnetism. You can boost this field by wrapping the conductor into a coil; more turns mean stronger field. Add a soft iron core inside and you've got an electromagnet that's switchable; electricity on, you get magnetism; switch off, magnetism's gone. This principle powers your starter motor solenoid, relays, and ignition systems. It's the bridge between electricity and magnetism that makes modern aircraft electrical systems work.
exam_tip: Current through wire = magnetic field. Coil = stronger field. Iron core = even stronger. Switchable = that's why it's useful. Know how this enables relays and solenoids.
key_facts: Current produces magnetic field around conductor
Coil strengthens magnetic field
Iron core further increases field strength
Switchable nature enables relays and solenoids
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Describe the lines of magnetic force around a; (a) straight conductor; (b) current carrying coil.
concept: Around a straight current-carrying wire, magnetic field lines form concentric circles perpendicular to the wire. Use the right-hand rule: thumb points along current direction, fingers curl showing field direction. A current-carrying coil strengthens this enormously—field lines bunch up through the centre, creating a pattern like a bar magnet with distinct north and south poles. That concentrated field is what makes solenoids and relay coils effective.
exam_tip: Right-hand rule for straight wire. Coil field = stronger and more concentrated. You might draw field patterns or explain directional effects.
key_facts: Straight wire: concentric circular field lines
Right-hand rule determines field direction
Coil: field lines concentrate through centre
Coil creates poles like a bar magnet
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Describe the lines of magnetic force around a; — (a) straight conductor
key_facts: [PENDING]

syllabus_desc: Describe the lines of magnetic force around a; — (b) current carrying coil
key_facts: [PENDING]

syllabus_desc: Describe the principle of operation of an electromagnetic switch (or relay).
concept: An electromagnetic switch or relay's essentially a remote control for electrical circuits. Current flows through a coil, creating a magnetic field that attracts an iron armature, which moves and operates contacts—opening or closing circuits without anyone touching anything. You've got a low-current control circuit that operates a high-current load circuit, which is brilliant for aircraft where you can't run heavy cables to every switch. Your starter motor uses a relay; so does your landing gear system.
exam_tip: Low current through coil creates magnetic field, attracts armature, operates contacts. Know why relays are essential in aircraft electrical systems (remote operation, circuit isolation).
key_facts: Coil carries control current
Magnetic field attracts armature
Armature movement operates contacts
Separates control from load circuit
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Describe typical examples of the use of electromagnetic switches in aircraft electrical circuits.
concept: You'll find electromagnetic switches throughout your aircraft. The starter solenoid's the obvious one—single switch in the cockpit controls a heavy-duty starter motor. Landing gear relays use electromagnets to divert power to retract or extend the gear. Landing light circuits often have relays so a small cockpit switch doesn't have to handle the full current. Fuel pump circuits, flap motors, heater fans—they all typically use relays. The beauty is you've got one small switch doing remote control work.
exam_tip: Be ready with specific examples: starter solenoid, landing gear relay, fuel pump relay. Explain why we don't just run heavy cables from cockpit switches.
key_facts: Starter motor solenoid
Landing gear relays
Landing light relays
Fuel pump control relays
Flap motor relays
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, AIC NZ

syllabus_desc: Explain the principle of electromagnetic induction.
concept: When you move a conductor through a magnetic field, or change the field around a conductor, you generate an electrical voltage. That's electromagnetic induction—Faraday's principle. The faster the field changes or the more field lines cut, the bigger the voltage generated. This is exactly how your alternator works: rotating magnets create a changing field through stationary coils, inducing voltage. It's also how your ignition magneto generates the high voltage for spark plugs, and how instrument pickups sense engine speed.
exam_tip: Movement or field change = induced voltage. Faster change = higher voltage. Know this is the basis for alternators and magnetos.
key_facts: Conductor moving through field generates voltage
Changing magnetic field induces voltage
Rate of change determines voltage magnitude
Foundation of alternator and magneto operation
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Describe the principle of operation of a simple alternator.
concept: A simple alternator's got a rotating magnetic field (rotor) inside stationary coils (stator). As the rotor spins, its magnetic field cuts through the stator coils, inducing AC voltage. The faster it spins, the higher the frequency. Your engine drives the alternator directly, usually through a belt. The frequency depends entirely on rotational speed and pole count. Modern aircraft alternators generate around 400 Hz at cruise RPM, which is then rectified to DC for the electrical system.
exam_tip: Rotating magnet, stationary coils, induced voltage. Higher RPM = higher frequency. This AC gets rectified to DC before reaching your battery.
key_facts: Rotor (magnet) rotates, stator (coil) stationary
Magnetic field change induces AC
Frequency increases with RPM
Output is AC before rectification
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Describe: (a) the features of a practical alternator; (b) how the AC output is normally rectified to provide DC.
concept: A practical alternator's more sophisticated than the simple version. It's got a three-phase output instead of single-phase, which gives smoother power. The rotor's an electromagnet (needs DC to excite it), the stator's typically delta-connected for efficiency. A voltage regulator controls excitation current to maintain constant output voltage regardless of load or RPM—critical for your electrical system. The AC output runs through a rectifier pack containing diodes; each diode lets current flow one direction only, converting AC to DC. Six diodes in a three-phase system gives near-constant DC output.
exam_tip: Practical alternators are three-phase, electromagnet rotor, voltage regulator. Rectifier uses diodes—know six diodes for three-phase. This is why your alternator output is smooth DC.
key_facts: Three-phase output for smoothness
Electromagnet rotor needs excitation
Voltage regulator maintains constant output
Rectifier pack uses diodes (typically 6)
Output is smooth DC for battery charging
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Describe: — (a) the features of a practical alternator
key_facts: [PENDING]

syllabus_desc: Describe: — (b) how the AC output is normally rectified to provide DC
key_facts: [PENDING]

syllabus_desc: Describe the principle of operation of a simple generator.
concept: A simple DC generator's similar to an alternator, but instead of rectifying AC, it uses a commutator—a split ring on the rotor that switches connections as it rotates. This switching action reverses which coil half's connected to the output at exactly the right moment, so output's always DC. You get pulsing DC rather than the smooth output of an alternator, but it's DC nonetheless. The faster you spin it, the higher the voltage. Older aircraft and some backup systems used generators; most modern ones switched to alternators for better output characteristics.
exam_tip: Generator uses commutator to switch coil connections, producing DC. Alternator + rectifier vs. generator + commutator—both end with DC but different methods.
key_facts: Rotating coil in magnetic field
Commutator switches connections
Output is DC (pulsing)
Voltage increases with rotation speed
Less efficient than modern alternators
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Describe: (a) the features of a practical generator; (b) how the output is passed through a commutator to provide DC.
concept: A practical DC generator's got a field coil system (electromagnets, not permanent magnets) for stronger output, and a commutator that's not just a simple split ring but a multi-segment ring with brushes making contact. Each segment connects to a different coil section; as the rotor turns, the brush arrangement ensures coil halves always connect to the external circuit at the moment their voltage's pointing the right direction. This gives relatively smooth DC, though still pulsing compared to modern alternators. The field coils need their own excitation circuit, usually from the generator's own output (self-excited).
exam_tip: Multi-segment commutator with brushes provides DC. Field coils generate magnetic field. Why did we move to alternators? Generators need field excitation, give pulsing output.
key_facts: Multi-segment commutator with brushes
Field coils provide magnetic field
Self-excited (output excites field)
Output is pulsing DC
Commutator ensures correct polarity switching
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Describe: — (a) the features of a practical generator
key_facts: [PENDING]

syllabus_desc: Describe: — (b) how the output is passed through a commutator to provide DC
key_facts: [PENDING]

syllabus_desc: Explain: (a) the need for voltage regulation for both alternators and generators; (b) why a generator also requires a current regulator and a reverse current relay.
concept: Both alternators and generators need voltage regulation because their output varies with RPM—cruise power's different from takeoff. You can't let battery voltage fluctuate all over the place; you need it stable around 28 volts. A voltage regulator senses output voltage and adjusts field excitation current to maintain constant voltage. Generators additionally need a current regulator because output current also varies with load, and you need to limit charge rate to protect the battery. They also need a reverse current relay that disconnects the generator when RPM drops below flight idle—prevents the battery back-driving the generator, which would burn out the brushes. Alternators, with their rectifier design, inherently prevent reverse current, so they don't need that relay.
exam_tip: Both need voltage regulation for constant output. Generators need current limiter and reverse current relay. Alternators don't—rectifier prevents reverse current. This is why alternators are superior.
key_facts: Voltage regulator needed (alternators and generators)
Maintains ~28 V regardless of load/RPM
Generators need current regulator
Generators need reverse current relay
Alternators prevent reverse current inherently
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain: — (a) the need for voltage regulation for both alternators and generators
key_facts: [PENDING]

syllabus_desc: Explain: — (b) why a generator also requires a current regulator and a reverse current relay
key_facts: [PENDING]

syllabus_desc: Explain the principle of operation of a battery.
concept: A battery works through chemical reaction. Inside, you've got two different metals (terminals) separated by chemical electrolyte. Chemical action causes electrons to build up at one terminal (negative) and leave the other (positive), creating voltage. When you connect an external circuit, electrons flow from negative to positive, powering your equipment. The chemical reaction continues supplying this electron imbalance until the chemicals are spent. In a secondary cell (rechargeable), applying external current reverses the chemical reaction, restoring the battery. Your aircraft's lead-acid battery stores energy chemically; the alternator, via the regulator, chemically recharges it.
exam_tip: Chemical reaction = voltage difference. Electrons flow negative to positive. Chemical depleted = dead battery. Recharge = reverse the chemistry. Simple concept, understand it deeply.
key_facts: Chemical reaction creates voltage
Two different terminals (positive/negative)
Electrolyte enables reaction
Electrons flow negative to positive externally
Rechargeable batteries reverse chemical reaction
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: With respect to batteries, distinguish between: (a) primary and secondary cells; (b) wet cells and dry cells; (c) lead-acid and nickel-cadium (and similar) types.
concept: Primary cells are one-shot; once discharged, you bin them (think torch batteries). Secondary cells recharge—your aircraft battery's secondary. Wet cells have liquid electrolyte (lead-acid: sulphuric acid) where you can actually see and sometimes top up the liquid; dry cells use paste electrolyte (NiCad: sealed unit). Lead-acid batteries are cheap, reliable, but heavier and need maintenance (checking water levels at Christchurch or Queenstown). NiCad batteries are lighter, don't spill, handle charge/discharge cycles better, but costlier and need special charging equipment. Most modern training aircraft still use lead-acid; some higher-spec birds use NiCad.
exam_tip: Primary = disposable. Secondary = rechargeable. Wet = liquid you see. Dry = sealed. Lead-acid = heavier, needs maintenance. NiCad = lighter, sealed, costlier. Know both types used in NZ aircraft.
key_facts: Primary cells: non-rechargeable
Secondary cells: rechargeable
Wet cells: liquid electrolyte (lead-acid)
Dry cells: paste electrolyte (NiCad)
Lead-acid cheaper, NiCad lighter and sealed
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: With respect to batteries, distinguish between: — (a) primary and secondary cells

syllabus_desc: With respect to batteries, distinguish between: — (c) lead-acid and nickel-cadium (and similar) types

syllabus_desc: Explain the meaning of: (a) battery capacity; (b) thermal runaway. Advisory Circular AC61-5 Revision 38 Sub Topic Syllabus Item
concept: Battery capacity is measured in ampere-hours (Ah)—it tells you how much current a battery can deliver over time. A 35 Ah battery can theoretically deliver 35 amps for one hour, or 17.5 amps for two hours, or 1 amp for 35 hours (though in practice it's not perfectly linear). This matters because if your alternator fails, you need to know how long your battery will keep essential systems running. Thermal runaway is the scary one, particularly with NiCad batteries. During charging, a NiCad's internal resistance drops as temperature rises—so it accepts more current, which generates more heat, which drops resistance further, which accepts even more current. It's a vicious cycle that can end with the battery overheating, venting toxic gases, or catching fire. Lead-acid batteries are less prone because their resistance increases with temperature, creating a natural self-limiting effect. That's why NiCad installations have dedicated temperature monitoring. If you see a battery temp warning in a helicopter with NiCad batteries, take it seriously—switch off the battery and land as soon as practical.
exam_tip: Battery capacity = ampere-hours (Ah). Thermal runaway = NiCad batteries, where rising temperature causes increasing charge acceptance in a runaway loop. Know why lead-acid is less susceptible (resistance increases with temp).
key_facts: Battery capacity: measured in ampere-hours (Ah). 35 Ah = 35 amps for 1 hour (theoretical). Thermal runaway: heat reduces internal resistance, increases charge current, generates more heat. Primarily a NiCad battery problem. Lead-acid less susceptible: resistance increases with temperature (self-limiting). NiCad installations require temperature monitoring. Thermal runaway can cause venting, toxic fumes, fire.
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the meaning of: — (a) battery capacity

syllabus_desc: Explain the basic features and operation of a: (a) lead-acid battery; (b) nickel-cadmium (NiCad) battery.
concept: Lead-acid batteries have lead plates (positive and negative) submerged in dilute sulphuric acid (electrolyte). Chemical reaction between lead and sulphuric acid creates voltage and current. Cells are wired in series—six 2-volt cells give you 12 volts (two batteries in series give 28 V). You need to check water levels because electrolyte can evaporate; if plates expose to air, battery's ruined. NiCad batteries use nickel oxide and metallic cadmium plates in potassium hydroxide electrolyte, sealed in a cell. No maintenance—no topping up. They handle overcharging better, accept fast charging, and work across wider temperature ranges. NiCad's more expensive upfront but longer-lasting and more reliable in harsh conditions.
exam_tip: Lead-acid: liquid electrolyte, needs maintenance, six cells in series for 12V. NiCad: sealed, no maintenance, better performance but pricier. Know both operational principles.
key_facts: Lead-acid: lead plates + sulphuric acid electrolyte
Six 2V cells in series = 12V
Requires water level checks
NiCad: nickel/cadmium plates + KOH electrolyte
NiCad sealed, maintenance-free, handles overcharge better
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the basic features and operation of a: — (a) lead-acid battery
key_facts: [PENDING]

syllabus_desc: Explain the basic features and operation of a: — (b) nickel-cadmium (NiCad) battery
key_facts: [PENDING]

syllabus_desc: Explain the advantages of NiCad batteries.
concept: NiCad batteries are sealed—no water to leak or evaporate, so zero maintenance. They're lighter than equivalent lead-acid (important in Ardmore training flights where weight matters). They accept rapid charging without damage, so if you've got a slow alternator or quick turnaround between flights, NiCad keeps up. Temperature range is wider; they work in freezing Christchurch winters and hot Queenstown summers without significant performance loss. They handle deep discharge and recharge cycles better—lead-acid sulphates up and dies if regularly discharged below 50%, but NiCad goes to zero without damage. Lifespan is longer. The main disadvantage is cost, but many operators reckon the maintenance savings and reliability make it worthwhile.
exam_tip: Advantages: sealed, lightweight, accepts fast charge, wide temperature range, handles deep discharge, longer lifespan. Why training schools might prefer them despite cost.
key_facts: Sealed—no maintenance or water checks
Lighter than lead-acid
Accepts rapid charging safely
Wide operating temperature range
Handles deep discharge/recharge cycles
Longer service life
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: State Pascal’s principle.
concept: Pascal's principle is fundamental to understanding hydraulic systems in your helicopter. It states that when you apply pressure to a confined fluid, that pressure transmits equally and undiminished in all directions throughout the fluid. Imagine a sealed container of oil with a piston on one end—push that piston and the pressure spreads evenly everywhere in the fluid, not just in the direction you're pushing. This is why hydraulic systems work so reliably: you can apply force at one point and get predictable, uniform pressure throughout. In your helicopter's hydraulic systems, whether you're operating the cyclic, collective, or flight control surfaces, you're relying on Pascal's principle. The fluid in the pipes doesn't compress (it's incompressible, unlike air), so that pressure transmits instantly and uniformly. That's what makes hydraulics so precise and responsive for flight controls.
exam_tip: Pascal's principle: pressure applied to confined fluid transmits equally in all directions. This is the foundation of why hydraulic systems work in helicopters.
key_facts: Pressure applied to confined fluid transmits equally in all directions
Fluid is essentially incompressible
Pressure acts perpendicular to all surfaces in contact with the fluid
Foundation principle for all hydraulic system operation
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Describe mechanical advantage, and how it can be gained hydraulically.
concept: Mechanical advantage in hydraulic systems comes down to the sizes of your pistons. If you've got a small piston pushing on one side of a confined fluid and a larger piston on the other, the larger piston will move with greater force but less distance. The mechanical advantage equals the ratio of the areas of the two pistons. For example, if your input piston is 10 square centimetres and your output piston is 50 square centimetres, you've got a 5-to-1 mechanical advantage. Push with 100 newtons on the small piston and you get 500 newtons pushing on the large one. This is exactly how your helicopter's hydraulic control systems work—small control inputs at the cyclic or collective lever generate large forces on the rotor head or flight control surfaces. The trade-off is always distance: to move that larger piston the same distance, you'd need to move the small piston five times further. Understanding this piston area ratio is crucial for grasping why hydraulic systems give pilots such precise, high-force control with relatively small inputs.
exam_tip: Mechanical advantage = ratio of piston areas. Larger output piston = greater force but less distance. This is why hydraulics let you control heavy loads easily.
key_facts: Mechanical advantage = output piston area ÷ input piston area
Greater piston area ratio = greater force multiplication
Force multiplication comes at the cost of distance (inverse relationship)
Small control inputs generate large control forces in helicopter systems
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the principle of operation of aircraft hydraulic services.
concept: Aircraft hydraulic systems work on Pascal's principle—pressure applied to a confined fluid is transmitted equally in all directions. You've got a small master cylinder (the input) connected via fluid lines to a larger slave cylinder (the output). Press on the small piston, the fluid transmits that pressure to the large piston. Since the large piston has more area, it produces more force—that's your mechanical advantage. In your helicopter, hydraulic systems boost the pilot's control inputs. Without hydraulic assistance, the aerodynamic loads on a helicopter's main rotor controls would be too heavy for the pilot to move comfortably, especially in larger machines. The system uses an engine-driven pump to pressurise hydraulic fluid (typically MIL-H-5606 mineral-based fluid), a reservoir to store fluid, a filter to keep it clean, an accumulator to smooth pressure pulses and provide emergency reserve, and actuators at each control—collective, cyclic, and sometimes tail rotor pedals. The R22 is simple enough to fly without hydraulics (it's actually designed that way), but the R44 and larger helicopters rely on hydraulic boost for normal control feel.
exam_tip: Pascal's principle: pressure transmitted equally through confined fluid. Hydraulic advantage = larger output piston area. Components: pump, reservoir, filter, accumulator, actuators. R22 = no hydraulic boost. R44+ = hydraulic-assisted controls.
key_facts: Pascal's principle: pressure applied to confined fluid transmitted equally in all directions. Small input piston → large output piston = mechanical advantage. Components: engine-driven pump, reservoir, filter, accumulator, actuators. Hydraulic fluid: typically MIL-H-5606 mineral-based. Helicopter use: boosts control inputs for collective, cyclic, pedals. Without hydraulics: control loads too heavy in larger helicopters. R22: designed without hydraulic boost. R44 and larger: require hydraulic assistance.
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the fundamental difference between piston and gas turbine (jet) engines.
concept: Piston engines work through reciprocating motion—pistons go up and down, driving a crankshaft that ultimately turns your propeller. Fuel and air burn in discrete pulses inside cylinders, each pulse pushing a piston down. Gas turbine engines work continuously—air flows through a compressor, fuel burns steadily in a combustor, and hot expanding gases drive turbines. Piston engines are efficient at lower speeds (why we use them for general aviation training at places like Ardmore), thermally efficient because of high compression ratios, and simpler mechanically. Turbines produce thrust directly, no propeller needed, but are fuel-hungry at low speeds and need expensive materials. For training aircraft with moderate power requirements, piston's the sensible choice.
exam_tip: Piston = reciprocating pistons, pulses of combustion, propeller. Turbine = continuous flow, hot gas drives turbines, direct thrust. Know why piston's used in your training aircraft.
key_facts: Piston: reciprocating motion, discrete combustion pulses
Turbine: continuous flow, continuous combustion
Piston: efficient low-speed, high compression ratio
Turbine: direct thrust, fuel-hungry at low speed
Training aircraft use piston for efficiency
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Distinguish between the basic types of piston aircraft engine.
concept: Aircraft piston engines come in several flavours. You've got in-line engines where cylinders line up in a row (older design, compact lengthwise but tall). Radial engines with cylinders arranged in a circle around a central crankshaft (old warbird types, rarely seen now). Flat or horizontally-opposed engines where cylinders are opposite each other (like your modern Lycoming or Continental—what you'll see in training twin at Hamilton or Christchurch). There's also V-engines with two banks of cylinders at an angle. For modern light aircraft, horizontally-opposed dominates because it's compact, good centre of gravity, and reasonable power-to-weight.
exam_tip: In-line, radial, flat, V-type. Know which one's in your aircraft. Most modern trainers = horizontally-opposed.
key_facts: In-line: cylinders in row
Radial: cylinders in circle around crankshaft
Flat/horizontally-opposed: opposite cylinders
V-type: two banks at angle
Modern trainers use horizontally-opposed
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Explain the fundamental operating principle of a piston four-stroke cycle engine.
concept: Four-stroke's the standard. First stroke (intake): piston moves down, intake valve opens, fuel-air mixture fills the cylinder. Second stroke (compression): both valves close, piston compresses the mixture, temperature and pressure rise. Third stroke (power/expansion): spark ignites the mixture, explosion pushes the piston down hard, producing useful work. Fourth stroke (exhaust): piston rises, exhaust valve opens, burnt gases exit. Then the cycle repeats. Each cylinder fires in sequence, so on a four-cylinder engine with proper timing, there's always one cylinder on the power stroke, giving continuous smooth delivery of power to the crankshaft. This is your light twin at Ardmore.
exam_tip: Intake, compression, power, exhaust. Know what happens in each. Spark timing happens during compression stroke, ignition during power stroke. Understand the thermodynamic sequence.
key_facts: Intake: valve open, mixture in
Compression: both closed, pressure rises
Power: spark ignites, explosion drives piston
Exhaust: valve open, gases out
Two complete crankshaft rotations = one cycle
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Explain the purpose of the main components of a four-stroke cycle piston engine including: (a) cylinders; (b) cylinder heads; (c) pistons; (d) connecting rods; (e) crankshaft; (f) valves; (g) valve operating mechanism; (h) camshaft; (i) spark plugs; (j) injectors.
concept: Cylinders are the pressure vessels where combustion happens—strong, usually cast aluminium. Cylinder heads seal the cylinder tops, house the spark plugs and valve seats, and create the combustion chamber shape. Pistons are the moving part inside cylinders, sealed with rings to prevent gas escape; they transfer combustion force to the connecting rod. Connecting rods link pistons to the crankshaft, converting up-down motion to rotational motion. The crankshaft's the main shaft transmitting power, turning at half the piston speed. Intake and exhaust valves let mixture in and gases out, operated by a camshaft (rotating shaft with lobes that push valve lifters). Spark plugs ignite the mixture at the right moment. In fuel-injected engines, injectors spray fine fuel mist into cylinders instead of carburettor mixing.
exam_tip: Understand the chain: mixture in via intake valve or injector, spark from plug ignites it, explosion pushes piston, connecting rod turns crankshaft, exhaust valve releases gases. Each component's purpose fits the cycle.
key_facts: Cylinders: pressure vessels for combustion
Heads: seal top, house plugs/valves
Pistons: transfer force to connecting rod
Connecting rods: convert linear to rotational motion
Crankshaft: rotates, transmits power
Valves: control intake/exhaust timing
Camshaft: operates valves via mechanical linkage
Spark plugs: ignite fuel-air mixture
Injectors: spray fuel (fuel injection engines)
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: With respect to a four-stroke piston engine, explain the meaning of the following terms: (a) cycle; (b) stroke; (c) top dead centre (TDC); (d) bottom dead centre (BDC); Advisory Circular AC61-5 Revision 38 Sub Topic Syllabus Item (e) bore; (f) clearance volume; (g) swept volume; (h) compression ratio; (i) firing interval; (j) firing order; (k) manifolds; (l) manifold pressure; (m) crank angle.
concept: Cycle's the complete four-stroke sequence repeated. Stroke is one piston motion (down or up), so four strokes = one cycle = two crankshaft rotations. Top dead centre (TDC) is the highest point the piston reaches—that's when spark happens. Bottom dead centre (BDC) is lowest. Bore is cylinder diameter, swept volume is the volume the piston displaces in one stroke (bore area × stroke length). Clearance volume is what's left at TDC—the combustion chamber. Compression ratio is swept plus clearance divided by clearance; higher ratios burn fuel more efficiently but need higher octane fuel. Firing interval's the crankshaft angle between successive cylinder fires (360 degrees divided by cylinder count for a four-cylinder). Firing order is the sequence cylinders fire in (usually 1-3-4-2 on a typical four-cylinder to balance forces). Manifolds are the piping—intake manifold distributes fuel-air mixture, exhaust manifold collects burnt gases. Manifold pressure is the pressure in the intake manifold, critical for power calculations. Crank angle is the crankshaft's position measured from TDC in degrees.
exam_tip: Definitions, definitions, definitions. These terms appear constantly in exam questions and when discussing engine performance. Know exactly what each means.
key_facts: Cycle = complete four-stroke sequence. Stroke = one piston motion. TDC/BDC = highest/lowest piston positions. Bore = cylinder diameter. Swept volume = bore area × stroke. Compression ratio = (swept + clearance) / clearance. Firing interval = 360 / cylinder count degrees. Firing order = sequence of ignition. Manifolds = intake/exhaust piping. Manifold pressure = pressure in intake. Crank angle = position from TDC.
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: With respect to a four-stroke piston engine, explain the meaning of the following terms: (a) valve lag; (b) valve lead; (c) valve overlap.
concept: Valve lag is when the exhaust valve doesn't open exactly when the piston reaches bottom dead centre (BDC)—it opens slightly after, so burnt gases still escape even as the piston starts up. Valve lead is when the intake valve opens before TDC on the intake stroke—it opens early while pressure's still expelling exhaust, creating turbulence that helps scavenge (clear) the cylinder. Valve overlap is the brief period where both intake and exhaust valves are simultaneously open—sounds wrong, but it's actually brilliant for gas flow. At the moment exhaust closes, the next piston downstroke creates negative pressure, and having the intake already cracked open lets fresh mixture rush in smoothly, improving scavenging and filling. It's a timing compromise; get it wrong and you waste mixture or trap exhaust.
exam_tip: Lag = opens late. Lead = opens early. Overlap = both open briefly. Understand why: exhaust scavenging and intake filling efficiency. Diagrams help here.
key_facts: Valve lag: exhaust opens after BDC
Valve lead: intake opens before TDC
Valve overlap: brief period both open
Purposes: scavenging exhaust, filling intake
Timing critical for efficiency
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

key_facts: [PENDING]

syllabus_desc: Explain the advantages of valve overlap.
concept: Valve overlap improves cylinder scavenging—removing residual burnt gases so the next intake stroke can fill with fresh mixture. When both valves are open briefly and the piston's near BDC, the outgoing exhaust creates pressure that pushes out the last bits of burnt gas, and the negative pressure from the descending piston draws fresh mixture in. This overlap creates a natural 'sweeping' action that improves gas exchange. Better scavenging means more fresh mixture per cycle, which means more power. Better filling means the engine operates more efficiently across the RPM range. Modern engines' camshaft profiles are optimised for this overlap; a wider overlap helps engine breathing at higher RPMs, though it can cause issues at very low idle speeds if not compensated for. That's why idle's rougher on some high-performance engines.
exam_tip: Advantages: improved scavenging, better cylinder filling, increased power output, better breathing at altitude. Know why engines with wide overlap might have rough idle.
key_facts: Improves scavenging of exhaust gases
Improves fresh mixture intake
Increases power output
Better engine breathing at altitude
Can cause rough idle if excessive
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Explain the meaning of the term ignition timing, and the need for spark advance.
concept: Ignition timing is when the spark occurs relative to the piston position. You can't wait until TDC to spark—combustion takes a few milliseconds to develop, and you need maximum pressure right after TDC (when the piston's driving downward). So spark happens before TDC, called 'spark advance.' Typically 20-30 degrees before TDC depending on the engine and operating conditions. At takeoff with high power, you want more advance; at cruise, less. Some older engines have manual advance/retard controls; modern ones adjust automatically. Spark too early and you get detonation (pre-ignition). Spark too late and combustion finishes after the power stroke starts, wasting energy. Getting it right is critical for smooth, efficient operation.
exam_tip: Spark at TDC? No, too late. Spark before TDC = spark advance. Why? Combustion needs time to build pressure. Understand the timing relationship to piston motion.
key_facts: Ignition timing = spark position relative to piston
Spark advance = occurs before TDC
Typical 20-30 degrees before TDC
Combustion takes milliseconds to develop
Too early = detonation, too late = power loss
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Distinguish between detonation and pre-ignition.
concept: Detonation and pre-ignition are two different abnormal combustion conditions, and you need to distinguish between them clearly. Detonation happens when the end gases—the fuel-air mixture at the far end of the combustion chamber—auto-ignite from the heat and pressure created by the normal flame front spreading from the spark plug. The pressure and temperature rise to the point where the remaining unburnt gas spontaneously ignites instead of burning in a controlled way. You'll hear a characteristic metallic knock or ping. Pre-ignition, by contrast, is when the fuel-air mixture ignites before the spark plug fires, triggered by a hot spot in the combustion chamber—a badly eroded piston, carbon deposit, or overheated valve. The timing's different, the cause is different, but both cause the same kind of damage. Both generate excessive pressure spikes that can crack pistons, damage bearing surfaces, and trash valves. In the air, if you suspect either condition, you've got to lean the mixture, reduce power, increase airspeed for cooling, or switch to a lower power setting immediately.
exam_tip: Detonation: end gases auto-ignite from pressure/heat during normal combustion. Pre-ignition: fuel-air mixture ignites before spark from hot spot. Both damage pistons and bearings.
key_facts: Detonation: unburnt end gases auto-ignite from pressure/temperature
Pre-ignition: mixture ignites before spark plug fires from hot spot
Detonation causes characteristic metallic knock/ping
Both conditions cause piston cracking, bearing damage, valve damage
Lean mixture, reduce power, increase airspeed to manage both conditions
source: Wagtendonk Principles of Helicopter Flight, Robinson R22 Manual

syllabus_desc: Explain the main causes of detonation (pre-ignition) including the reasons for avoiding them.
concept: The main causes of detonation (which is sometimes called pre-ignition by pilots, though technically that's different) centre on conditions that raise combustion temperature and pressure excessively. High manifold air pressure—pushing more air into the engine—increases the pressure and temperature when that mixture ignites, increasing the risk of the end gases auto-igniting. A lean fuel-air mixture burns hotter than a rich one, raising combustion temperature dangerously. High ambient temperature or a hot engine already heat-soaked makes it worse. Low-octane fuel has a lower resistance to auto-ignition, so using fuel below your engine's rating invites trouble. Spark advance that's too aggressive—igniting the mixture too early—means the pressure and temperature rise to dangerous levels before the piston's in the right position. Accumulated carbon deposits in the combustion chamber act as hot spots and raise compression, pushing temperature higher. The damage from detonation is severe: you can crack pistons, destroy bearing surfaces, burn valves, and wreck your engine in minutes. In the air, if you suspect detonation, lean the mixture to cool combustion, reduce power to lower manifold pressure, increase airspeed to cool the engine, and use higher-octane fuel if available.
exam_tip: Causes of detonation: high MAP, lean mixture, high temps, low octane, spark advance too far. Damage includes piston cracking, bearing failure, valve damage.
key_facts: High manifold air pressure increases combustion pressure/temperature
Lean mixture burns hotter, increasing detonation risk
High engine temperature and high ambient temperature increase risk
Low-octane fuel has lower auto-ignition resistance
Spark advance that's too aggressive increases combustion pressure
Detonation causes piston cracking, bearing damage, valve damage
Management: lean mixture, reduce power, increase airspeed, use higher octane
source: Wagtendonk Principles of Helicopter Flight, Robinson R22 Manual

syllabus_desc: Explain diesel (compression ignition) knock (detonation).
concept: Diesel engines (compression ignition engines) operate completely differently from petrol engines, and so does the problem of diesel knock. In a compression-ignition engine, there's no spark plug. Instead, you compress the air so heavily that the heat of compression alone—typically 500-600°C—ignites the fuel when it's injected into the combustion chamber. The problem arises if there's too long a delay between fuel injection and ignition. During that delay, more and more fuel accumulates in the chamber, and when ignition finally happens, all that fuel burns at once in a sudden, violent explosion rather than a controlled burn. This rapid pressure rise causes the characteristic diesel knock—a heavy metallic clang. Diesel knock happens when injection timing is retarded, when fuel quality's poor (low cetane rating), or when combustion chamber deposits interfere with the ignition process. The knock itself is less damaging than petrol engine detonation, but it's a sign of poor running and inefficient combustion. Modern diesel engines have high-precision fuel injection systems that control timing extremely accurately to prevent knock.
exam_tip: Diesel knock: in compression-ignition engines, if ignition delay is too long, accumulated fuel causes rapid pressure rise when ignition occurs, creating heavy knock.
key_facts: Compression-ignition engines rely on heat of compression (500-600°C) to ignite fuel
Ignition delay too long = fuel accumulates before ignition
Rapid pressure rise from sudden combustion causes diesel knock
Diesel knock is characteristic heavy metallic clang
Caused by retarded injection timing, poor fuel quality, combustion chamber deposits
Less damaging than petrol engine detonation but indicates poor combustion
source: Wagtendonk Principles of Helicopter Flight, Robinson R22 Manual

syllabus_desc: Describe the use of a Ground Power Unit (GPU).
concept: A Ground Power Unit, or GPU, is an external electrical power source that lets you start your helicopter without relying solely on the aircraft battery. It's essentially a large, portable power pack—either a cart with a diesel engine driving an alternator, or a solid-state power supply—that you plug into your electrical system. The GPU provides the high current your starter motor demands, which can be 300+ amps momentarily. If you tried to pull that much current from your onboard battery in cold weather, you'd drain it flat within seconds, and a drained battery won't support starting. A GPU solves this problem beautifully, especially in cold climates where battery performance drops significantly. It also lets you run electrical systems—instruments, lights, avionics—while parked without draining the battery. At Christchurch or Hamilton on a cold winter morning, a GPU is your friend. Most helicopter operations at larger bases like Ardmore or Queenstown have GPUs available. Using one is simple: connect the GPU to the aircraft's external power socket, switch the battery selector to GPU (or ensure the system draws from GPU), and you've got full electrical power for starting without battery stress.
exam_tip: GPU provides external electrical power for engine starting. Prevents battery drain, essential in cold weather. Connect to external power socket before starting.
key_facts: GPU is external electrical power source (cart or solid-state unit)
Provides high current for starter motor (300+ amps)
Prevents battery drain during starting, especially in cold weather
Allows electrical systems to run while parked without depleting battery
Connection via external power socket on aircraft
Common at larger helicopter bases in NZ
source: Wagtendonk Principles of Helicopter Flight, Robinson R22 Manual

syllabus_desc: Explain the basic principle of operation of a simple float-type carburettor.
concept: A float carb's old-school but brilliant. Fuel enters a float chamber (a reservoir) where a floating cork or foam ball sits on the fuel surface. As fuel level rises, the float rises with it, mechanically closing a needle valve that shuts off fuel inflow. Fuel level stays constant around a set level. Below the float chamber is a venturi—a narrowed section in the airflow—where the airspeed increases and pressure drops. This low pressure sucks fuel from the float chamber through a jet (small hole) into the airstream, where it mixes with air. The faster the airflow, the more fuel gets sucked in. Throttle controls the overall airflow; you open it for more power. Simple, mechanical, no electrics needed. You'll still see these on older trainers, especially singles at Ardmore or Christchurch. Fuel injection's replaced them on modern planes.
exam_tip: Float controls fuel level. Venturi creates low pressure. Jet sprays fuel. Throttle controls airflow. Understand the three-way control: float holds level, venturi sucks fuel, throttle sets power.
key_facts: Float chamber maintains fuel level
Venturi creates low pressure zone
Pressure drop sucks fuel through jet
Airspeed controls fuel quantity
Throttle controls total airflow
Mechanical, no electrical components
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, AIP NZ

syllabus_desc: Explain the following in relation to fuel-air mixture ratios: (a) rich and lean; (b) normal workable mixture ratio limits; (c) the chemically correct or stoichiometric ratio; (d) the approximate ratios for maximum power output and best economy.
concept: Mixture ratio is the weight of fuel to weight of air. Rich means more fuel, lean means less fuel (more air relative to fuel). The stoichiometric ratio is the 'perfect' chemical blend: about 15 kilos of air to 1 kilo of fuel, where every fuel molecule has exactly enough oxygen to burn completely. In practice, you operate somewhere between about 8:1 (very rich) and 18:1 (very lean). For maximum power, you want about 12:1—richer than stoichiometric because excess fuel cools the combustion, preventing detonation and allowing more power without knock. For best economy (saving fuel), you want closer to 18:1—very lean—because there's plenty of oxygen and less fuel burned for the same power. The catch: too lean and you lose power and risk detonation; too rich and the mixture won't burn completely, wastes fuel and fouls spark plugs. At takeoff, you typically enrich to prevent knock. At cruise, you lean to save fuel. Every altitude and power setting has an optimal mixture.
exam_tip: Stoichiometric = perfect chemical burn (rarely used in practice). Max power = rich (~12:1). Best economy = lean (~18:1). Understand the tradeoffs.
key_facts: Ratio = fuel weight : air weight
Stoichiometric ≈ 15:1 (theoretical perfect)
Max power ≈ 12:1 (rich of stoichiometric)
Best economy ≈ 18:1 (lean)
Operating range ~8:1 to 18:1
Too rich = waste, fouling; too lean = power loss, detonation
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the following in relation to fuel-air mixture ratios: — (a) rich and lean
key_facts: [PENDING]

syllabus_desc: Explain the following in relation to fuel-air mixture ratios: — (b) normal workable mixture ratio limits
key_facts: [PENDING]

syllabus_desc: Explain the following in relation to fuel-air mixture ratios: — (c) the chemically correct or stoichiometric ratio
key_facts: [PENDING]

syllabus_desc: Explain the following in relation to fuel-air mixture ratios: — (d) the approximate ratios for maximum power output and best economy
key_facts: [PENDING]

syllabus_desc: Describe a typical carburettor mixture setting curve and the operating area where detonation will occur.
concept: A carb mixture curve shows how engine power varies with mixture ratio at a given RPM and altitude. If you plot it, you get a peak—maximum power at a particular mixture ratio (usually around 12:1, rich of stoichiometric). As you lean from that peak, power drops gradually on the economy side. As you enrich past the peak, power also drops because excess fuel cools combustion and increases weight without burning. The detonation zone is the lean side of the power peak—as you lean out, you're running hotter and risking knock. If conditions are hot (high ambient temperature, high power), the detonation zone starts earlier (richer). This is why at high altitude where it's cold, you can lean further without knock. A typical operating curve shows a safe envelope where you can run without detonation; outside that, knock risk rises sharply.
exam_tip: Power peak at rich side of stoichiometric. Lean side = economy. Detonation zone = lean side of power peak. Know why temperature and altitude shift the zone.
key_facts: Power peaks around 12:1 (rich of stoichiometric)
Leaning past peak = economy but losing power
Detonation zone on lean side of peak
High temperature pushes detonation zone richer
High altitude (cold) allows leaner mixture
Safe operating envelope defined by detonation margin
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the need for the following in an aero-engine carburetor; (a) a venturi; (b) atomization and diffusion; (c) an accelerating system; Advisory Circular AC61-5 Revision 38 Sub Topic Syllabus Item (d) an idling system; (e) a power enrichment (economizer) system; (f) a mixture control; (g) a cut-off system.
concept: The venturi in a carburettor is a narrow throat section that accelerates the airflow. As air speeds up through the venturi (Bernoulli's principle), its pressure drops. The fuel discharge nozzle sits right in this low-pressure zone, so fuel gets sucked out of the float chamber and into the airstream. Faster airflow = lower pressure = more fuel drawn out. It's a beautifully simple metering mechanism. Atomisation is breaking that fuel into tiny droplets—the smaller the droplets, the more surface area exposed to air, and the better the fuel vapourises and mixes. A well-atomised mixture burns efficiently and evenly. A poorly atomised mixture (big droplets) burns unevenly, wastes fuel, and can cause rough running. Vaporisation is the final step—the liquid droplets turn to vapour as they absorb heat from the air and the intake manifold. Complete vaporisation is the goal because only fuel vapour burns properly. Mixture distribution is getting that vapourised fuel-air mixture to all cylinders equally. Poor distribution means some cylinders run rich while others run lean—one cylinder could be overheating while another is fouling its plugs.
exam_tip: Venturi: accelerates air, drops pressure, draws fuel. Atomisation: breaks fuel into tiny droplets. Vaporisation: liquid to vapour. Distribution: equal mixture to all cylinders. These are sequential steps in getting fuel from float chamber to combustion.
key_facts: Venturi: narrow throat accelerates airflow, pressure drops (Bernoulli). Fuel discharge nozzle in low-pressure zone. Faster air = lower pressure = more fuel drawn. Atomisation: breaking fuel into tiny droplets. More surface area = better vaporisation. Vaporisation: liquid droplets → fuel vapour. Only vapour burns efficiently. Mixture distribution: equal fuel-air to all cylinders. Poor distribution: some cylinders rich, others lean.
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the disadvantages of a float type carburettor in an aero-engine.
concept: Float carbs struggle with the variable g-forces in aircraft. A hard turn, rough turbulence, or inverted flight can slosh the fuel around in the float chamber, causing the level to rise or fall erratically. The fuel supply to the engine becomes inconsistent. Carburettor ice is another killer—as fuel evaporates in the venturi, the pressure drop cools the fuel. In humid conditions around freezing, ice can form and block the venturi, starving the engine. Modern carb heaters help, but it's still a threat. Float carbs also suffer from poor distribution of fuel across multiple cylinders—some cylinders get richer mixture than others, leading to uneven running. They're altitude-sensitive because the fuel flow's driven by air pressure drop; at higher altitude where air's thinner, the venturi effect weakens and fuel flow reduces (mixture leans) even without you touching anything. They're slow to respond to sudden power changes—that accelerator pump does its best, but lag is noticeable. Fuel injection solved most of these problems, which is why it's now standard on modern aircraft.
exam_tip: Float carbs have: slosh issues in manoeuvres, carb ice risk, poor fuel distribution, altitude sensitivity, slow response. Know why fuel injection's better.
key_facts: G-forces cause fuel slosh and inconsistency
Carburettor ice risk in humid cold
Poor fuel distribution between cylinders
Altitude sensitivity (mixture leans automatically)
Slow throttle response
Why industry moved to fuel injection
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the correct use of the mixture control.
concept: The mixture control adjusts how much fuel flows relative to air, allowing you to maintain proper ratio at different altitudes. At sea level (Ardmore), the air's dense and you typically fly at full rich—plenty of fuel because there's plenty of air. As you climb and air gets thinner, the venturi effect weakens and the mixture leans. If you didn't adjust, you'd end up too lean at altitude. So you lean out the mixture control—reducing fuel flow—to maintain the proper ratio. The rule of thumb: climb to altitude, reduce power, lean gradually by pulling the mixture knob while watching the engine response (rough slightly, then smooth again). At cruise you lean for economy. If you're on final approach and descending into denser air, you should enrich the mixture again or the engine might run rough. Never go full lean in flight except to shut down at the end. Proper leaning extends range, prevents plug fouling, and keeps the engine happy. Improper use (too rich or too lean) wastes fuel or risks detonation.
exam_tip: Altitude changes = mixture adjustment. Climb = lean for altitude. Descend = enrich as you enter denser air. Full rich for takeoff. Never abuse mixture.
key_facts: Adjusts fuel-to-air ratio for altitude
Full rich at takeoff (sea level)
Lean gradually during climb
Proper cruise leaning extends range
Enrich when descending into denser air
Prevents fouling and detonation if used correctly
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, AIP NZ

syllabus_desc: Explain the consequences of operating with over-rich and over-lean mixture settings.
concept: Over-rich (too much fuel): engine runs rough and black-smoky exhaust trails behind you. Power drops because the excess fuel cools the combustion process and not all fuel burns completely. Spark plugs foul with carbon deposits. Fuel consumption goes through the roof. At extreme richness, the engine can flood and quit entirely. Over-lean (too little fuel): initially you might get a slight power increase (leaning toward best power), but go too far and combustion temperatures soar because there's less fuel to absorb heat. Detonation risk increases dramatically. The engine runs rough, you might hear metallic knocking, and cylinder head temperatures climb past limits. Exhaust gas temperatures peak and then start dropping as the engine struggles. At extreme lean, the engine overheats, valves can burn, and the engine can seize. The sweet spot is different for different phases of flight—full rich for takeoff and climb (cooling), leaned for cruise (efficiency), full rich again for descent (cooling and ready for go-around power). In a helicopter, mixture management matters just as much—your R22's Lycoming runs the same way.
exam_tip: Over-rich: rough running, black smoke, fouled plugs, high fuel consumption, power loss. Over-lean: high CHT, detonation risk, rough running, potential engine damage. Know the correct mixture setting for each phase of flight.
key_facts: Over-rich: rough running, black exhaust smoke. Carbon fouling of spark plugs. Increased fuel consumption. Power loss from incomplete combustion. Over-lean: increased combustion temperatures. Detonation risk increases. Rough running, metallic knocking. High CHT, potential valve burning. Engine seizure at extreme lean. Correct settings: full rich for takeoff/climb, leaned for cruise, full rich for descent/go-around.
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, AIP NZ

syllabus_desc: With respect to carburettor ice, explain the process and atmospheric conditions for the formation of: (a) refrigeration (or fuel) ice; (b) throttle ice; (c) impact ice.
concept: Carburettor ice forms through two mechanisms. First, fuel vaporisation cooling: when liquid fuel vapourises in the venturi and throttle area, it absorbs heat from the surrounding metal, dropping temperatures by as much as 20-30°C. Second, the venturi effect itself causes air temperature to drop as pressure drops (adiabatic cooling). Together these can produce freezing conditions inside the carburettor even when outside air temperature is well above zero—hence the danger zone of roughly -5°C to +20°C OAT with visible moisture or high humidity. Ice forms on the throttle butterfly valve and venturi walls, progressively restricting airflow. Throttle ice is the sneakiest because it forms at low power settings (partially closed throttle) when the gap around the butterfly is smallest—exactly the conditions you have during a descent or cruise. The atmospheric conditions most conducive are: temperatures between -5°C and +20°C, high relative humidity (above 50%), and visible moisture (cloud, rain, fog). Descending through moist air on a mild day at reduced power is the classic carb icing scenario. In NZ, that describes a lot of approaches into places like Hamilton or Christchurch on a typical autumn morning.
exam_tip: Two cooling mechanisms: fuel vaporisation (20-30°C drop) and adiabatic cooling in venturi. Danger range: -5°C to +20°C OAT. Worst conditions: low power, high humidity, visible moisture. Throttle ice forms at partial throttle opening.
key_facts: Carb ice formation mechanisms: fuel vaporisation cooling (20-30°C temperature drop) and adiabatic cooling in venturi. Temperature drops can produce ice even with OAT well above freezing. Danger zone: -5°C to +20°C OAT with high humidity. Ice forms on throttle butterfly and venturi walls. Throttle ice: worst at low/partial power settings. Most conducive conditions: mild temperatures + high humidity + visible moisture. Descending at reduced power through moist air = classic scenario.
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, AIP NZ

syllabus_desc: Explain the: (a) normal symptoms of carburettor ice formation; (b) the correct use of the carburettor heat control.
concept: Carb ice sneaks up on you. The first symptom in a fixed-pitch propeller aircraft is a gradual RPM drop—the ice restricts airflow through the carburettor venturi, reducing power output. In a constant-speed prop, you'll see manifold pressure drop first (the governor masks the RPM change initially). Engine may run rough as ice partially blocks the fuel-air passage. In severe cases, the engine quits entirely. The correct response: apply full carburettor heat immediately. Hot air from the exhaust manifold is ducted into the carburettor intake, melting the ice. Initially the engine will run even rougher—that's the melted ice water passing through as liquid—and RPM may drop further. Don't panic and remove the heat; leave it on. Within 30-60 seconds, the ice clears and engine performance recovers. Use full heat, not partial—partial heat can actually make things worse by raising the temperature into the most dangerous icing range without melting existing ice. Apply carb heat before entering known icing conditions (descent, humid days, temperatures between -5°C and +20°C). In a helicopter, carb ice is particularly dangerous because you can't glide—you're going into autorotation immediately if the engine quits.
exam_tip: Symptoms: RPM drop (fixed pitch), MAP drop (constant speed), rough running. Treatment: full carb heat, expect initial roughness, leave it on. Never use partial heat. Icing range: -5°C to +20°C. Especially dangerous in helicopters.
key_facts: Carb ice symptoms: gradual RPM drop (fixed pitch), MAP drop (constant speed). Rough running as ice restricts airflow. Engine can quit in severe cases. Treatment: apply FULL carburettor heat immediately. Initial response: engine runs rougher (melted water). Leave heat on until engine recovers (30-60 seconds). Never use partial heat—can worsen icing. Icing conditions: -5°C to +20°C, humid air, descent power settings. Carb ice especially dangerous in helicopters (immediate autorotation if engine quits).
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Describe how carburettor icing can prevent the normal operation of an aero- engine.
concept: Carburettor icing is a silent killer—it can develop without any drama or obvious warning, and it can shut down your engine completely. It happens because the fuel-air mixture in a carburettor undergoes evaporative cooling. The fuel evaporates, which absorbs heat from the surrounding air, and if that air is moist (which it usually is), water vapour condenses. In cold enough conditions, that condensed moisture freezes into ice crystals. These ice crystals accumulate around the venturi (the narrow bit that accelerates air) and around the throttle valve. As ice builds up, it progressively restricts the air flowing into the engine. The engine starts running rough because it's getting a richer mixture—the same amount of fuel but less air. Power drops noticeably. If ice continues to accumulate unchecked, eventually the venturi and throttle can become completely blocked. Airflow stops. The engine leans out dramatically, misfires, sputters, and quits. You're in the air with a dead engine and no obvious reason why—you didn't lean it too much, you didn't mess with fuel flow, the engine just stopped. Carburettor icing can develop in ambient temperatures from -10°C up to about 10°C, worst around 0 to -5°C. Prevention is applying carburettor heat before entering icing conditions. Detection is the symptoms: rough running, power loss, engine roughness.
exam_tip: Carb ice blocks venturi and throttle, restricts airflow, causes rough running and power loss. Can cause complete engine failure. Apply carb heat before icing conditions.
key_facts: Forms from evaporative cooling of fuel-air mixture and condensation of water vapour
Accumulates around venturi and throttle valve, restricting airflow
Engine runs rough initially as mixture richens (less air, same fuel)
Progressive power loss if not corrected
Can cause complete engine failure if blockage becomes total
Most likely between -10°C and +10°C ambient temperature, worst near 0°C
Prevention: apply carburettor heat before entering icing conditions

syllabus_desc: Describe the effect of excessive carburettor heat in high output engines.
concept: Excessive carb heat is hot air drawn from around the exhaust manifold, and in high-power engines (or at high power settings), that air can be extremely hot—potentially 300°C or more. If you leave carb heat on continuously at high power, you're running an abnormally hot mixture. This increases combustion temperature significantly, raising detonation risk. You also lose air density benefit (hot air's less dense, less oxygen per volume), so power actually decreases. Additionally, excessive heat can warp or damage carb components, potentially causing fuel vaporization in the lines (vapor lock) where fuel boils before reaching the carb, interrupting supply. Most engines are designed to use carb heat only when needed for ice prevention, not continuously. The rule: use carb heat only in icing conditions or immediately after landing where carb ice risk is highest. Continuous carb heat on high-power flight is a recipe for detonation problems and potential component damage. Some high-performance engines even have carb heat limiting devices to prevent misuse.
exam_tip: Excessive carb heat in high power = detonation risk, power loss, potential vapor lock. Use carb heat only when needed for ice, not routinely in cruise.
key_facts: Hot air from exhaust can exceed 300°C
Raises combustion temperature
Increases detonation risk at high power
Reduces air density (power loss)
Can cause vapor lock in fuel system
Use only for ice prevention, not routinely
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: With respect to carburettor air intakes, explain the correct use of: (a) ram air; (b) filtered air; (c) carburettor heat.
concept: Ram air is the air forced into the carb by forward motion; at speed, this ram pressure actually increases air density entering the carb, giving you more oxygen and better power. Most aircraft have a ram air intake that uses this ram effect. Filtered air is the normal intake route, passing through an air filter to remove dust, sand, and other contaminants before the carb. You'd use filtered air when flying in dust storms or sandy conditions, though filtered air introduces a small pressure drop (resistance). Carburettor heat is hot air from the exhaust, used only for icing prevention. The logic: normally you want ram air (extra oxygen at speed) through a filter (cleanliness). In icing conditions, you switch to carb heat to warm the carb and prevent ice. You can't have both simultaneously—it's either ram air or carb heat at any given moment. At Queenstown, if you're flying low and slow on approach, you might lose the ram effect anyway; that's when carb heat becomes essential for ice prevention. During takeoff and climb at high power, you want ram air through filter. During descent or in known icing conditions, you might apply carb heat.
exam_tip: Ram air = ram pressure boost, better power. Filtered air = protection from contaminants. Carb heat = ice prevention. Choose based on conditions.
key_facts: Ram air: forward motion increases pressure/density
Provides power boost at speed
Filtered air: passes through intake filter
Protects engine from contamination
Carburettor heat: for ice prevention
Can't use ram air and carb heat simultaneously
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge
