syllabus_desc: Explain the meaning of: (a) thermal efficiency; (b) mechanical efficiency; (c) volumetric efficiency.
concept: Thermal efficiency, mechanical efficiency, and volumetric efficiency are three separate measures of how well an engine converts fuel into useful power, and they're critical concepts for understanding engine performance limitations. Thermal efficiency is the percentage of the fuel's heat energy that actually becomes mechanical work. In a typical piston aircraft engine, that's roughly 30-35%. The rest escapes as heat through the exhaust and cooling system. No piston engine is ever going to be more efficient than that theoretical limit due to the laws of thermodynamics. Mechanical efficiency refers to friction losses within the engine itself. The crankshaft spins, bearings and pistons slide, accessories run—all of that creates friction that absorbs power. A well-maintained engine achieves roughly 80-90% mechanical efficiency, meaning 80-90% of the power produced by combustion actually reaches the propeller or, in your case, the rotor shaft, while 10-20% is lost to internal friction. Volumetric efficiency is the amount of air actually flowing into the cylinders compared to the theoretical amount if the intake system were perfectly efficient. At sea level, a well-designed engine achieves roughly 100% volumetric efficiency or slightly better. But as altitude increases and air density decreases, volumetric efficiency drops dramatically. At 10,000 feet, the air's only about 70% as dense as sea level, so volumetric efficiency might drop to 70%, meaning fewer oxygen molecules per cylinder, less power available. Superchargers and turbochargers are specifically designed to restore volumetric efficiency at altitude by compressing the incoming air back to higher density.
exam_tip: Thermal (~30-35%): fuel heat to work. Mechanical (~80-90%): friction losses. Volumetric (~100% sea level, drops at altitude): actual air vs theoretical.
key_facts: Thermal efficiency: percentage of fuel heat converted to mechanical work (~30-35%)
Mechanical efficiency: accounts for friction losses inside engine (~80-90%)
Volumetric efficiency: actual air mass vs theoretical maximum
At sea level, volumetric efficiency ~100% for well-designed engines
At altitude, volumetric efficiency drops as air density decreases
At 10,000 ft, air density ~70% of sea level, reducing available power
Superchargers/turbochargers restore volumetric efficiency at altitude
source: Wagtendonk Principles of Helicopter Flight, FAA Helicopter Handbook

syllabus_desc: Explain the relationship between typical brake power and power available curves.
concept: A brake power curve plots engine power (vertical axis) against RPM (horizontal axis). At low RPM, power's low. As RPM increases, power increases (more combustion cycles per minute). The curve typically rises steeply initially, then levels off, reaching a peak somewhere around cruise RPM, then might drop slightly or plateau at maximum RPM. A power-available curve (often used in aircraft performance analysis) shows how much power the propeller can absorb at various airspeeds and altitudes. At low airspeed, the propeller demands little power; as airspeed increases (denser air loading the propeller), power required increases dramatically. The intersection of available power (what the engine delivers) and power required (what the propeller needs at that airspeed) determines performance. Below critical altitude, maximum brake power's available at wide-open throttle. Above critical altitude, power drops despite full throttle. In level flight, the aircraft climbs when power available exceeds power required, descends when power required exceeds available, and flies level when they balance. Understanding both curves helps predict aircraft performance across the speed envelope and altitude.
exam_tip: Brake power curve: engine capability vs RPM. Power available: what propeller absorbs vs airspeed. Intersection determines performance balance.
key_facts: Brake power increases with RPM
Peaks near cruise RPM
Power available: propeller demand at each speed
Climb: available exceeds required
Level flight: balanced
Descent: required exceeds available
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the general conditions for the most efficient engine operation.
concept: Most efficient operation occurs at cruise power and altitude, where the engine's running at moderate settings (not maximum power, not low power), mixture's properly leaned, and ignition's optimally timed. Specific conditions: manifold pressure set to the value specified for cruise (typically 60-75% power), RPM set for the desired speed-efficiency trade-off (lower RPM = less fuel burn but slower; higher RPM = more speed but more fuel), mixture leaned to the smooth setting just before the power peak (around 17:1 to 18:1 ratio), engine temperature in the green band (not too hot, not too cold), and smooth flying (no excess maneuvring or attitude changes that waste energy). Avoid high-power climbs and descents that stress the engine and waste fuel. Smooth power transitions are more efficient than abrupt throttle changes. Flying at altitude (thinner air but cooler) often provides better efficiency than sea level (denser but hotter). Every engine's different; the POH specifies cruise power settings for best economy or best range. Following the POH exactly gives the best efficiency. Essentially: steady-state moderate-power flight with properly-leaned mixture.
exam_tip: Efficient operation: cruise power, altitude, leaned mixture, smooth flying. Avoid high power and abrupt changes.
key_facts: Cruise power (not max, not low)
Proper altitude (cooler, thinner air)
Mixture leaned to optimum
Smooth flying, minimal maneuvring
Engine temps in green band
POH-specified cruise settings
Steady-state operation
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Describe the effect of changing altitude on manifold pressure and power output of normally aspirated engines. Ancillary Systems
concept: A normally-aspirated engine (no supercharger or turbo) draws intake air directly from the atmosphere. As altitude increases, atmospheric pressure decreases, so air density decreases. The engine draws the same volume of air per stroke, but that volume contains fewer oxygen molecules. Manifold pressure (measured on the aircraft's manifold pressure gauge, which shows intake pressure) decreases with altitude. At sea level, manifold pressure might read 28-30 inches of mercury at full throttle. At 10,000 feet, it might read only 18 inches even with throttle wide open. Less air means less combustion force, so brake power decreases. The power decrease is roughly proportional to air density—at 10,000 feet (about 70% sea-level density), you'd have roughly 70% of sea-level power. This is why aircraft can't climb indefinitely; at some critical altitude, power available equals power required (no excess for further climb), and the aircraft reaches its service ceiling. Pilots manage this by descending power settings at altitude (e.g., 'at cruise, reduce to 65% power') to match what the atmosphere allows. Above critical altitude, the manifold pressure gauge becomes a power limiter rather than a throttle—you can't exceed the power available regardless of throttle position.
exam_tip: Higher altitude = lower air density = lower manifold pressure = lower power. Effect: power drop roughly matches density drop.
key_facts: Manifold pressure decreases with altitude
Air density decreases with altitude
Power output decreases proportionally
Roughly 70% power at 10,000 ft
Above critical altitude, power's density-limited
Climb performance worsens higher
Service ceiling: power available = power required
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Describe the systems which typically require DC power in an aircraft.
concept: Your DC electrical system is the backbone of the aeroplane. Everything that needs power before you get the engines running—and quite a bit after—relies on that battery and alternator. Think about what happens when you're doing your walk-around at Ardmore or anywhere else: you need lights, you need the radio to talk to ATC, the fuel pump needs juice to get fuel to the engines. Once you're running, systems like landing lights, navigation lights, instrument panel lighting, the autopilot, and the entire avionics suite all depend on DC power. Even your flaps and landing gear on many twins use electrically-driven pumps or motors. The point is, without DC power, you're not flying very far.
exam_tip: List the major consumers: battery charging/starting, lighting (cockpit, nav, landing), radio, fuel pump, engine instruments, autopilot, landing gear/flaps, and avionics. Focus on what's essential for flight ops.
key_facts: DC power comes from the battery and alternator/generator
Critical systems: radio, fuel pump, engine instruments, lighting
Landing gear and flaps often use electric motors or pump drives
Autopilot and modern avionics require steady DC supply
Loss of electrical power means loss of radio comms and engine instrumentation

syllabus_desc: Explain the function(s) of the following in a typical electrical system: (a) the battery; (b) a ground power source; (c) the alternator or generator; (d) bus bars; (e) over voltage protection.
concept: Your battery is your electrical starter motor and emergency backup—it cranks the engine and provides power until the alternator takes over. Once you're running, the alternator (or generator on older twins) generates AC current, which gets rectified to DC to charge the battery and power the system. Bus bars are just distribution points—think of them as electrical junctions where power splits off to different circuits. A ground power unit (GPU) does the same job as your battery for cold starts, especially useful in winter at places like Christchurch. Over-voltage protection is critical because if your alternator fails in a way that causes over-charging, you'll cook your battery and damage sensitive avionics. That's why you've got a voltage regulator watching the output.
exam_tip: Remember: battery starts and backs up; alternator powers and charges; bus bars distribute; GPU is external battery; voltage regulator prevents over-charge. Be ready to explain why over-voltage protection matters (equipment damage, battery boil-off).
key_facts: Battery provides starting power and emergency backup
Alternator/generator produces main electrical power in flight
Bus bars are distribution centres for electrical loads
Ground power unit (GPU) provides external power for starting
Voltage regulator prevents over-voltage damage to equipment

syllabus_desc: Given appropriate data, determine the duration of battery operation following a generator failure. Advisory Circular AC61-5 Revision 38 Sub Topic Syllabus Item
concept: If your alternator fails mid-flight, you're running on battery power alone—and that battery's capacity is finite. You've got your battery amp-hour rating (typically 25-35 Ah on a light twin) and the aircraft's electrical load in amps. Simple division: capacity divided by load equals remaining time. So if you've got a 30 Ah battery and your electrical load is pulling 40 amps (lights, radio, instruments), you've got roughly 45 minutes before that battery's flat. But here's the reality: you'll start dropping non-essential loads, shed the landing light, maybe dim the panel—so you can stretch it. The exam will give you specific numbers, so the maths is straightforward, but the practical lesson is urgent: get the aircraft on the ground at the nearest suitable airfield.
exam_tip: Use the formula: time (hours) = battery capacity (Ah) ÷ electrical load (amps). In the real world, you'll reduce non-essential loads immediately after an alternator failure and declare an emergency with ATC.
key_facts: Battery capacity is rated in amp-hours (Ah)
Calculate remaining time: capacity ÷ load = hours
Typical light twin battery: 25–35 Ah
Cockpit load in normal flight: 20–50 amps depending on configuration
Reduce non-essential loads immediately (landing lights, autopilot, non-essential avionics)

syllabus_desc: Describe the relative advantages and disadvantages of: (a) a generator; (b) an alternator.
concept: Generators are the old-school option: they're DC straight out, they're robust, and they tolerate overloads better. But they're heavier, bulkier, and they drop output as engine RPM drops—bad news for idling and low-speed flight. Alternators are lighter, more efficient, and produce constant voltage output across a wide RPM range, which is why modern aircraft use them. They can handle high electrical loads at any engine speed. The downside? They're more sensitive to overloads and high ambient temperatures. On a long hot day flying out of Queenstown with everything running, an alternator will work harder, but it'll work. A generator might drop out on you at low RPM, and that's a headache you don't need on approach.
exam_tip: Generators: robust, older, RPM-dependent output, heavier. Alternators: modern, efficient, constant output, lighter, temperature-sensitive. Know why you'd choose each.
key_facts: Generators produce DC directly; alternators produce AC then rectify to DC
Generators output decreases with lower engine RPM
Alternators maintain constant voltage across wide RPM range
Alternators are lighter and more efficient
Generators better tolerate electrical overloads; alternators more sensitive

syllabus_desc: Describe the relative advantages and disadvantages of: — (a) a generator

syllabus_desc: Describe the relative advantages and disadvantages of: — (b) an alternator
key_facts: [PENDING]

syllabus_desc: Explain the functions and interpretation of: (a) left-zero ammeters; (b) centre-zero ammeters.
concept: An ammeter tells you whether your electrical system is charging or discharging. A left-zero ammeter has zero on the left, negative (discharge) on the left, and positive (charge) on the right. When you're in flight with the alternator running normally, the needle should sit somewhere on the positive side—usually around +20 to +40 amps depending on the load. A centre-zero ammeter is symmetrical: zero in the middle, discharge to the left, charge to the right. Some twins have this setup. The key thing is: if you see a persistent discharge on either gauge during normal flight, your alternator's not keeping up or it's failed. And if the needle jumps erratically, you've got a problem brewing. Watch it on startup—there's always a brief charge surge as the battery tops up.
exam_tip: Left-zero: zero on left side; centre-zero: zero in centre. Know which one indicates charge (positive) and discharge (negative), and what normal inflight reading looks like (small positive charge).
key_facts: Left-zero ammeter: negative left, zero left edge, positive right
Centre-zero ammeter: negative left, zero centre, positive right
In normal flight: small positive charge (+10 to +40 amps typical)
Discharge indication during flight means alternator failure or overload
Erratic needle movement indicates system malfunction

syllabus_desc: Explain the functions and correct operation of a: (a) single battery master switch; (b) split battery/alternator switch.
concept: A single battery master switch is simple: it disconnects the entire electrical system from the battery when you switch it off. That's your primary control—kill it before you leave the aircraft, otherwise you'll come back to a flat battery. A split battery/alternator switch is more sophisticated and found on some twins. You can shut down the alternator independently while keeping the battery online, or vice versa. Why would you do this? If your alternator's running rough and drawing excessive current, you can switch it out and go battery-only until you land. Or if you've got an electrical fire or short circuit, you might kill the alternator but keep the essential battery-backed systems alive long enough to get down safely. The split switch gives you more control, but single switches are easier to manage—fewer decisions in an emergency.
exam_tip: Single switch: kills everything. Split switch: allows independent control of battery and alternator. Know when you'd use each in an electrical emergency (fire, alternator failure, over-charge condition).
key_facts: Single battery master: one switch cuts all electrical power
Split switch: independently control battery and alternator circuits
Always OFF before leaving the aircraft
Split switch useful for electrical fire or alternator malfunction
Some aircraft have battery-only essential bus for critical systems

syllabus_desc: Explain the functions and correct operation of a: — (a) single battery master switch

syllabus_desc: Explain the functions and correct operation of a: — (b) split battery/alternator switch

syllabus_desc: Explain the operation of: (a) fuses; (b) circuit breakers; (c) overload switches.
concept: Fuses are one-shot protectors: wire them into a circuit, and if the current exceeds the fuse rating, the wire melts and the circuit opens. Once it's blown, you need a new fuse. Circuit breakers are reusable: they have a bimetal strip that bends with heat from excess current and pops open automatically. Cool it down, hit the reset button, and you're back in business—assuming the problem's fixed. Overload switches are similar but designed for specific applications like the starter motor: they'll cut out if current spikes too high. The philosophy is protection: you don't want a short circuit melting through your wing harness and starting a fire. A blown fuse or popped breaker is telling you something's wrong; don't just keep resetting and hoping—investigate.
exam_tip: Fuses: one-time use, melt on overload. Breakers: reusable, pop open, can reset. Never swap a fuse for a higher amp rating or bypass it. If it keeps popping, there's a fault—don't ignore it.
key_facts: Fuses: disposable wire element that melts on overcurrent
Circuit breakers: reusable, reset manually or automatically
Overload switches protect high-current circuits (starter)
Each circuit protected by correctly-rated fuse or breaker
Repeated fuse blows indicate underlying electrical fault

syllabus_desc: Explain the operation of: — (a) fuses

syllabus_desc: Explain operational principles for the handling of the electrical DC system, including: (a) avoiding overheating electrical services if operated during pre-flight; (b) not starting or stopping the engine with unnecessary electrical equipment switched on; (c) avoiding prolonged use of the starter motor; (d) checking satisfactory operation of the alternator/generator after start, and periodically during flight; (e) ensuring the battery master is switched off before vacating the aircraft after flight; (f) use of a ground power unit.
concept: Here's the practical stuff you'll actually do every flight. During pre-flight, don't run the avionics and lights for ages on battery power alone—you'll heat things up and flatten the battery before you even start. When you're starting the engine, kill the unnecessary stuff: strobes, autopilot, non-essential avionics. Let the starter do its job without fighting to supply 10 other systems. Don't hold the starter engaged for more than 30 seconds at a time—you'll fry it and the battery. As soon as the engine fires and stabilises, check your ammeter: you should see a positive charge, meaning the alternator's doing its job. Keep an eye on it in cruise and before descent too. Before you hop out after landing, flip the battery master to OFF—leave it on and you'll come back to a completely flat battery. And if you're dealing with a cold-soak start in winter at Christchurch or Hamilton, a GPU gets you warm electrons without hammering your onboard battery.
exam_tip: Pre-flight: minimal electrical load on battery. Startup: non-essentials OFF, starter no more than 30 seconds, check ammeter immediately. Landing: switch OFF before you go. Know why each step matters.
key_facts: Limit pre-flight electrical load to protect battery state
Reduce electrical load before engine start (landing lights, autopilot, non-essentials OFF)
Starter motor: maximum 30 seconds continuous use
Check ammeter shows positive charge within 30 seconds of start
Periodic inflight check of alternator output and system voltage
Always switch battery master to OFF before leaving the aircraft
GPU reduces battery stress in cold weather or repeated starts

syllabus_desc: Explain the identification of: (a) an excessive charge rate; (b) alternator/generator failure; (c) a total electric failure.
concept: An excessive charge rate—usually more than 50–60 amps on a normal twin—tells you your voltage regulator's not working or the alternator's in a fault mode. Your ammeter will show a sustained high charge, and if you let it run, it'll cook your battery and fry your avionics. Alternator failure is usually obvious: ammeter swings to a discharge reading and stays there, your lights dim, and your battery voltage starts dropping. You'll see it within minutes of the failure. Total electrical failure is the worst case: ammeter dead, flight instruments slowly failing, no radio, nothing. That means both the alternator and battery are offline or the main bus is disconnected. In all cases, you need to declare an emergency, get to the nearest suitable aerodrome, and land as soon as safely possible. You'll need to navigate by compass, fly basic instruments, and manage without radio—not ideal, but it's doable.
exam_tip: Excessive charge: ammeter high (>50A), voltage rising dangerously. Alternator failure: ammeter discharge, dimming lights, sinking voltage. Total failure: nothing works electrically. Each has different implications for your emergency procedures.
key_facts: Excessive charge rate: >50–60 amps sustained charge is abnormal
Alternator failure: ammeter shows discharge, lights dim, voltage drops
Total electrical failure: no instruments, no radio, compass-only navigation
Each failure has distinct ammeter and lighting indications
Declare emergency with ATC immediately upon detection
Land at nearest suitable aerodrome without delay

syllabus_desc: Explain the risks associated with: (a) resetting blown fuse/popped circuit breakers; (b) using fuses as a switch.
concept: Resetting a fuse or breaker without investigating the underlying fault is like putting a plaster over a broken bone. You'll get airborne, and that fault—a short circuit, an overloaded wire, whatever it is—is still there. You hit cruise altitude, and suddenly the whole system fails or something catches fire. A popped breaker or blown fuse is a symptom; you need to diagnose and fix the cause, not just reset and hope. Using a fuse as a switch is even worse: fuses aren't designed to open and close repeatedly. The contacts degrade, you can cause arcing, and you'll develop resistance in the circuit which causes heating. Plus, a deliberate fuse operation might not give you a clean open—it can stick or partially engage. Always use the proper switch for on/off control.
exam_tip: Never reset a blown fuse without diagnosing the problem. Never use a fuse as a switch. Both practices risk fire, electrical failure, and aircraft loss.
key_facts: Blown fuses/popped breakers indicate an underlying electrical fault
Resetting without investigation leads to recurrence and potential fire risk
Fuses degrade with repeated opening and closing
Using fuses as switches causes contact damage and resistance heating
Always diagnose the root cause before resetting a protection device

syllabus_desc: Explain the issues related specifically to ‘technically enhanced aircraft’ (TEA) during the handling of electrical malfunctions. Advisory Circular AC61-5 Revision 38 Sub Topic Syllabus Item
concept: Technically Enhanced Aircraft (TEA) present unique electrical system challenges. TEA aircraft feature glass cockpits, integrated avionics suites, and advanced autopilot systems that increase electrical demand and complexity. During electrical failures, TEA aircraft may lose primary flight displays, navigation capability, and automated systems simultaneously. Pilots must understand degraded mode operations, know which instruments remain available on backup power, and be able to revert to basic 'steam gauge' flying if needed. Battery endurance in TEA is critical because more systems depend on electrical power than in conventional aircraft.
exam_tip: TEA = glass cockpit aircraft with high electrical dependency. Know what you lose in an electrical failure and how to manage degraded modes. Backup battery duration is critical.
key_facts: TEA = Technically Enhanced Aircraft (glass cockpit)
Higher electrical demand than conventional aircraft
Electrical failure can lose PFD, MFD, autopilot simultaneously
Must know degraded mode operations
Battery endurance more critical in TEA
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, Principles of Helicopter Flight (Wagtendonk)

syllabus_desc: Distinguish between gravity-fed and pump-fed fuel systems.
concept: Fuel systems come in two basic flavours: gravity-fed and pump-fed. The fundamental difference is how the fuel gets from the tank to the engine. In a gravity-fed system, the fuel tank is mounted higher than the engine—at least two to three feet of elevation difference. Gravity—simple physics—feeds fuel downward into the carburettor. No mechanical or electrical pump is needed. The system is simple, reliable, and requires no power to operate. Some light helicopters and very basic aircraft use gravity feed. The disadvantage is that it strictly limits where you can mount the fuel tank. You need that elevation, which sometimes conflicts with aircraft design and weight distribution. In a pump-fed system, an engine-driven pump (mechanically driven by the engine itself) or an electric boost pump (powered by the aircraft battery) supplies the fuel under pressure to the engine. Pump-fed systems are far more flexible: you can mount fuel tanks anywhere—low, high, wherever you need for weight distribution and structural efficiency. You can easily feed multiple tanks to a single engine. The system is more reliable in manoeuvring flight because mechanical or electrical pumps maintain consistent fuel pressure regardless of aircraft attitude. If you're rolling, diving, or pulling G-forces that would interrupt gravity feed, a pump maintains pressure. Most modern helicopters, including training helicopters like the R22, use pump-fed systems. The engine-driven pump provides main fuel flow during normal operation, and an electric boost pump provides fuel for engine starting and backup.
exam_tip: Gravity-fed relies on tank height above engine. Pump-fed uses engine or electric pump. Pump systems more flexible, reliable in manoeuvring.
key_facts: Gravity-fed: tank mounted higher than engine, fuel flows downward
Gravity-fed advantage: simple, reliable, no power required
Gravity-fed disadvantage: strictly limits tank mounting location
Pump-fed: mechanical (engine-driven) or electric pump supplies pressure
Pump-fed advantage: flexible tank location, works in any aircraft attitude
Pump-fed: more reliable in manoeuvring due to maintained pressure
Most helicopters use pump-fed systems (engine pump + electric boost pump)
source: Wagtendonk Principles of Helicopter Flight, FAA Helicopter Handbook

syllabus_desc: For a typical fuel system, explain the function, and where appropriate, the correct handling of the following: (a) fuel strainers and filters; (b) engine-driven fuel pump; (c) boost (auxiliary) pump(s); (d) engine primers and priming systems.
concept: A typical helicopter fuel system has several key components working together to get clean fuel reliably from the tank to the engine. Fuel strainers and filters are your first defence against contamination. There's typically a coarse strainer in the tank or just below the tank outlet—this catches large particles before fuel enters the main system. Then there's a fine filter between the tank and the engine, often in the fuselage under the cowling—this catches anything smaller. Both need periodic inspection and cleaning; a clogged filter can starve the engine of fuel. The engine-driven fuel pump is the workhorse during normal flight. It's mechanically driven by the engine's accessory gearbox and provides constant fuel pressure to the fuel injection system or carburettor. This pump operates continuously when the engine's running and requires no external power—if your alternator fails, the engine-driven pump still works. However, it can't prime the engine from a dead stop. That's where the electric boost pump comes in. It's powered by the aircraft's electrical system and can be selected on before engine start, getting fuel pressure built up before the engine-driven pump takes over. After engine start, you can usually select the boost pump off in normal flight, though some aircraft leave it running. In some systems, the boost pump automatically takes over if the engine-driven pump fails. The primer is a small manual pump that enriches the fuel-air mixture for cold starts. You work the primer handle before starting, injecting extra fuel directly into the engine cylinders to help ignition when the engine's cold and air density is low. Each component has a specific role, and understanding what each does is essential for reliable operation.
exam_tip: Strainers/filters remove contaminants. Engine pump provides main fuel pressure. Boost pump for starting/backup. Primer enriches mixture for cold start.
key_facts: Fuel strainers/filters remove particles (coarse and fine stages)
Engine-driven pump mechanically provides main fuel pressure
Engine pump works even if alternator fails (no electrical dependence)
Electric boost pump provides pressure for starting and backup
Boost pump usually selected off after engine start during normal flight
Primer manually enriches mixture for cold engine starting
All components critical for reliable fuel delivery and engine operation
source: Wagtendonk Principles of Helicopter Flight, FAA Helicopter Handbook

syllabus_desc: For a fuel system, explain the function, and where appropriate, the correct handling of the following: (a) tank filler caps and drains; (b) expansion spaces; (c) tank vents; (d) baffles; (e) sumps and drains; (f) fuel quantity detectors; (g) fuel strainers and filters; (h) tank selector valves; (i) cross feed valves; (j) fuel flow meters.
concept: Tank filler caps seal the system and prevent contamination during refuelling—use the correct cap for each tank and check the O-ring seals. Tank drains are at the lowest point; you'll open them during pre-flight to sample fuel and check for water and sediment. Expansion spaces let fuel grow as temperature rises without rupturing the tank—that's why you don't fill tanks completely on a hot day. Tank vents allow air in as fuel is consumed so you don't create a vacuum that collapses the tank or starves the engine. Baffles are internal dividers that prevent fuel sloshing around in rough air and keep the fuel pickup submerged even in a dive or steep turn. Sumps are collection points at the tank bottom where sediment and water settle; you drain these during pre-flight. Fuel quantity detectors (usually floats) tell you how much fuel's in each tank—check them on the ground and monitor them in flight. Tank selectors let you choose which tank feeds the engine; cross-feed valves let you transfer fuel between left and right tanks if one's running low or if an engine fails. Fuel flow meters show fuel consumption rate—essential for managing endurance and detecting abnormal consumption.
exam_tip: Know each component's function and when to check it (pre-flight sampling, tank selection during flight, monitoring quantity). Understand why expansion space matters and what happens if vents are blocked.
key_facts: Filler caps: sealed, O-ring seals checked, correct cap per tank
Tank drains: lowest point, sampled pre-flight for water and sediment
Expansion space: allows fuel thermal expansion without tank rupture
Tank vents: prevent vacuum and maintain pressure as fuel is used
Baffles: prevent fuel sloshing, keep pickup submerged in manoeuvres
Sumps: collection points for sediment and water, drained pre-flight
Quantity detectors: float types, check ground and monitor in flight
Tank selector: choose fuel source; cross-feed: transfer between tanks
Fuel flow meter: shows consumption rate for endurance planning

syllabus_desc: Describe: (a) the general rules for refuelling; (b) the correct use of fuel tank dipsticks; (c) the correct use of magna-sticks.
concept: Refuelling rules: aircraft nose into wind to prevent fuel spray, engine off (obviously), master switch off to prevent sparks, ground the refuelling cart and aeroplane to prevent static discharge. Fill on a level surface and don't overfill—leave the expansion space. Check the fuel type matches your engine (Mogas vs avgas, octane rating, etc.). Dipsticks are old-school but still used: you insert them into the filler neck until they bottom, then pull them out and read the fuel level against the graduated marks. They're accurate but they're a physical inspection, not electronic—you've got to do it. Magna-sticks (or fuel strut sticks) are plastic dipsticks with floating magnets inside. You fill the stick with a sample of fuel from the tank drain, and magnets at different levels show you the water level. If water's present, it settles at the bottom and the magnet sinks to it. The marks tell you the fuel grade and any water contamination. It's faster than waiting for sediment to settle, and it gives you confidence the fuel's clean. Some FBOs still prefer the traditional jar method—letting fuel sit in a clear container so water settles visibly—but magna-sticks are becoming standard.
exam_tip: Refuelling: level surface, nose into wind, ground the aircraft, no spark sources, correct fuel type. Dipstick: insert fully, read graduated marks. Magna-stick: fill from tank sample, note magnet position for water and fuel grade.
key_facts: Refuelling procedure: level surface, nose into wind, engines off, master off
Ground aircraft and refuelling cart to prevent static discharge
Confirm fuel type and octane rating before refuelling
Don't overfill; leave expansion space
Dipstick: insert to bottom, read graduated scale on withdrawal
Magna-stick: fill with fuel sample, magnets show water level and fuel grade
Fuel should be clear and bright; contamination requires tank draining

syllabus_desc: Describe the correct procedures to be used for carrying out fuel quality checks.
concept: Fuel quality checks are simple but non-negotiable. At refuelling, you've already verified the grade. On pre-flight, you'll drain a sample from the lowest sump point of each tank into a clear container (usually a fuel sampler cup at the refuelling point). Let it sit for a few seconds; look for water settling at the bottom—it'll be cloudy or a distinct layer. Check for sediment (particles, rust, debris) which should be absent. The fuel should be clear and bright, with the correct colour for the grade: Avgas 100LL is typically light blue, Avgas 100 is green, Jet A is clear to pale straw. Any discolouration, cloudiness, or sediment means the tank needs flushing or fuel dumping. On a multi-tank aircraft like a twin, check every tank—you might find contamination in one wing tank but not the other. If you find water, don't fly; declare the aircraft unserviceable and contact maintenance. This isn't a shortcut—it's the difference between a normal flight and an engine failure at low level.
exam_tip: Sample every tank pre-flight. Look for water (cloudiness at bottom), sediment (particles), and correct colour. Cloudy fuel = no-go. Know the colour standards for fuel grades used in NZ.
key_facts: Drain fuel sample from lowest sump point of each tank
Sample into clear container; let settle briefly
Look for water (cloudiness), sediment (particles), and correct colour
Avgas 100LL: light blue; Avgas 100: green; Jet A: clear to straw
Any cloudiness or sediment means tank requires maintenance
Check every tank on multi-tank aircraft
Fuel contamination is a no-go condition

syllabus_desc: Describe the indications of water in a fuel sample.
concept: Water in fuel is the enemy. It'll corrode fuel system components, it'll plug injectors and filters, and it can freeze in the fuel lines at altitude, blocking fuel flow mid-flight. When you drain a fuel sample and water's present, you'll see a distinct layer at the bottom of the container—it looks cloudy or milky if it's emulsified (mixed with fuel), or it might be a clear layer below the fuel. Tiny droplets suspended throughout the fuel make it look hazy or turbid. On a hot day, you might see no water initially, but as the sample cools in your hand, water that was vapour condenses out and becomes visible. If you let the sample sit for a minute, water will settle to the bottom and you'll see it clearly. The colour changes too: normal fuel is bright and clear; fuel with water is dull, cloudy, or has a distinct separation. In serious contamination, you might even smell the water—it has that distinct odour. Don't take any chances: if you see water, the aircraft doesn't fly, and you need to get maintenance involved to find the source (usually a leaking fuel cap or a tank vent failure in damp conditions).
exam_tip: Water indicators: cloudiness, haze, distinct bottom layer, colour change (dull instead of bright). Let sample sit to let water settle. Any visible water = no-go.
key_facts: Water appears as cloudiness, haze, or distinct layer at bottom of sample
Water may be emulsified (mixed throughout) or separated (bottom layer)
Cool sample in hand; water vapour condenses as temperature drops
Fuel with water looks dull or turbid instead of bright and clear
Serious contamination has visible water column at bottom
Water in fuel risks filter blockage, injector damage, and fuel line freeze
Any visible water is a no-go condition

syllabus_desc: Describe the symptoms of a blocked fuel tank vent in a gravity feed fuel system.
concept: A blocked vent in a gravity-feed system creates a vacuum in the tank as fuel is consumed. The engine will run fine at first, but as fuel level drops, the vacuum gets stronger and eventually strangles the fuel flow. You'll notice the engine running rough, losing power, and if it's bad enough, cutting out completely. The problem is it's intermittent—climb or descend slightly, maybe the siphoning effect changes, and fuel flows again. You might chalk it up to carburettor ice or fuel starvation from leaning the mixture. But the real giveaway is that fuel quantity gauge starts fluctuating wildly because the float is bouncing around as the vacuum sucks it. If you suspect a blocked vent mid-flight, you might try a gentle climb or descent to change the pressure differential, or you might open a cabin air vent to let air into the cabin, which could relieve some back-pressure. But really, it's a land-and-sort-it situation. On the ground, check that the vent isn't clogged with dirt, ice, or a blocked valve.
exam_tip: Blocked vent symptoms: engine rough or cutting out as fuel level drops, fluctuating fuel gauge, progressive power loss. On the ground, check vent for blockage or ice.
key_facts: Blocked vent creates vacuum as fuel is consumed
Engine roughness and power loss as vacuum builds
Fuel quantity gauge fluctuates erratically
Problem worsens as fuel level drops (more vacuum)
Changing altitude may temporarily relieve symptom
Vents can block with dirt, ice, or internal failure
Land immediately if blocked vent suspected

syllabus_desc: Describe the importance of correct management of fuel selection.
concept: On a twin-engine aeroplane, you've got multiple fuel tanks—typically left wing, right wing, and maybe a centre tank. You've got a selector valve on the flight deck that lets you choose which tank feeds each engine. Get this wrong and you'll have an engine failure, or worse, an asymmetric thrust situation where one engine's running and the other's starved. Proper fuel management means: know your tank capacities, monitor which tank is supplying each engine, understand your fuel consumption rates, and plan your takeoff sequence. Some aircraft require you to burn from specific tanks in a specific order to maintain centre of gravity. If you're flying out of Hamilton and you've got an asymmetric load (say, full right tank and half-full left), you need to be deliberate about which tank feeds which engine so you don't end up with both engines trying to feed from the depleted side. On some operations, you'll cross-feed in flight to balance consumption. And critically, if one engine dies, you need to immediately switch that engine to an alternate tank (if available) or declare the situation and plan your approach. Sloppy fuel selection has killed a lot of pilots—it's not a details-to-ignore kind of thing.
exam_tip: Know your tank layout and capacities. Plan takeoff fuel sequence. Monitor consumption. Maintain CG balance. If one engine dies, switch to alternate fuel source immediately. Document fuel selection in your pre-flight checklist.
key_facts: Multi-tank aircraft require deliberate tank selection
Wrong selection causes single-engine failure or dual failure
Tank selector valves must be properly identified and used
Fuel consumption must be monitored to maintain aircraft balance
Asymmetric fuel loads require cross-feed planning
Engine failure due to fuel selection requires immediate alternate tank selection
Fuel management is part of pre-takeoff and in-flight checklist

syllabus_desc: Detail the actions recommended in the case of loss of power through faulty fuel selection.
concept: You've selected the wrong tank, one engine's lost power, and suddenly you're flying an asymmetric twin—not a fun situation. First thing: don't panic. Immediately check your fuel selector: it's probably in the wrong position. Switch it to the correct tank, and usually the engine will pick up within 5–10 seconds as fuel pressure builds. If the engine doesn't respond, try the opposite tank if you've got alternatives, or try your crossfeed valve if available. If you've got an engine failure that you can't recover—the selector's already in the right position and fuel's available elsewhere—then you're declaring a single-engine emergency with ATC. You've lost asymmetric thrust, so trim carefully, reduce power on the good engine to maintain control, and plan your descent to the nearest suitable aerodrome. Call it early so ATC can vector you and prepare the airfield. Don't try to climb or maintain altitude on one engine in a twin with a significant loading—you'll just burn fuel inefficiently and stress the airframe. Get down safely.
exam_tip: Wrong fuel selection = engine loss. Immediately check and correct selector position. Verify fuel's actually in that tank (gauge might show empty). If engine doesn't recover, declare emergency and plan landing at nearest suitable aerodrome. Manage asymmetric thrust carefully.
key_facts: Check fuel selector immediately when engine power is lost
Switch to correct tank; engine usually responds within 5–10 seconds
Confirm fuel exists in the selected tank (check gauge)
Try crossfeed if available
If unrecovered, declare single-engine emergency
Plan descent to nearest suitable aerodrome
Manage asymmetric thrust: reduce good engine power, trim carefully
Don't attempt to climb on one engine unnecessarily

syllabus_desc: Describe the typical methods of cross-feeding fuel.
concept: Cross-feeding is transferring fuel from one tank to another, usually from a wing tank to the opposite wing tank. On many twins, you've got a crossfeed selector valve that lets you feed a single engine from the opposite wing tank. Why would you do this? If one wing tank's running low and the other's still full, you can cross-feed to balance consumption and maintain centre of gravity. If an engine's on the verge of failure and you've got fuel in the opposite tank, you can try cross-feeding to see if the problem's a blocked feed line instead of total fuel starvation. Some aircraft have manual crossfeed valves; others have electric selectors on the panel. The procedure is simple: move the crossfeed selector to 'on' or to the desired position, monitor that the engine stays running, and watch your fuel gauges to ensure you're actually consuming from the opposite tank. Cross-feeding during cruise helps balance aircraft loading on long flights. Some operations require specific cross-feed sequences—check your flight manual. And remember: you can only cross-feed if you've got fuel in both tanks and the crossfeed system is serviceable. You can't pull fuel from an empty tank.
exam_tip: Crossfeed transfers fuel between left and right tanks. Used to balance consumption and maintain CG. Operate selector, monitor engine and fuel gauges. Confirm fuel actually transfers. Know your aircraft's crossfeed valve location and operation.
key_facts: Crossfeed transfers fuel between wing tanks to maintain balance
Valve is either manual or electric selector on flight deck
Monitor fuel gauges to confirm crossfeed operation
Engine should stay running; observe no fluctuation
Used during cruise for extended flights to manage CG
Can help if one feed line is blocked (try crossfeed to opposite tank)
Requires fuel in both tanks; can't transfer from empty tank
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Explain the main functions of an engine oil system. Advisory Circular AC61-5 Revision 38 Sub Topic Syllabus Item
concept: Engine oil is absolutely vital to engine operation—it's far more than just a lubricant. An oil system serves five interconnected functions, and failure of any one can destroy an engine rapidly. Lubrication is the obvious one: oil creates a slippery film between moving parts—pistons sliding in cylinders, crankshaft spinning in bearings, camshaft lobes sliding against tappets. Without this film, metal-to-metal friction would generate heat so intense that the engine would seize within seconds. Cooling is the second function. Engine oil absorbs heat from the engine's hot spots and carries it away to the oil cooler (or in simpler systems, to the air flowing around the engine). Combustion temperatures exceed 2000°C in the cylinders; oil carries away excess heat so components don't overheat. Cleaning is the third function. As the engine runs, combustion byproducts and wear particles accumulate. The oil carries these contaminants—suspended as a slurry—back to the oil filter, where the filter removes them. Without this cleaning action, sludge and debris would accumulate and block passages. Sealing is the fourth function. Piston rings are the primary seal between the combustion chamber and the crankcase, but they're not perfect. Gas molecules leak past the rings, a process called blowby. The oil's viscosity helps seal these tiny gaps, preventing excessive blowby and keeping pressure in the combustion chamber where it belongs. Corrosion protection is the fifth function. Oil contains additives—detergents, dispersants, anti-wear compounds, and corrosion inhibitors—that protect metal surfaces from oxidation and acid attack, especially during cold starts when moisture is present. All five functions must work together for engine health.
exam_tip: Oil lubricates, cools, cleans (removes wear particles), seals cylinders (reduces blowby), protects against corrosion.
key_facts: Lubrication: reduces friction between moving parts
Cooling: absorbs and carries away heat from hot spots
Cleaning: carries combustion byproducts and wear particles to filter
Sealing: reduces blowby past piston rings
Corrosion protection: additives protect from oxidation and acid attack
Each function essential—failure of one can destroy engine
Oil additives include detergents, dispersants, anti-wear, inhibitors
source: Wagtendonk Principles of Helicopter Flight, FAA Helicopter Handbook

syllabus_desc: Describe the effect of temperature on the viscosity and lubrication qualities of oil.
concept: Oil viscosity is its thickness, and it changes dramatically with temperature. Cold oil is thick and sluggish—on a winter morning at Christchurch, you might have to wait longer before the oil pressure stabilises because thick oil doesn't flow easily through the bearings. Hot oil is thin and watery—high altitude or a long descent in summer heat makes the oil fluid, which is good for flow but bad for the film between bearing surfaces. The viscosity needs to be in a sweet spot: thick enough to maintain a protective film between bearings (low viscosity = poor lubrication = bearing wear), thin enough to flow and circulate (high viscosity = poor flow = pressure drop and cooling problems). That's why you use specific oil grades: a SAE 40 oil stays relatively consistent across temperature ranges, while a SAE 10 oil is thin and flows better in winter but might be too thin in summer. Modern ashless dispersant oils (used in aviation piston engines) maintain viscosity better across temperature swings than straight mineral oil, which is why you use them.
exam_tip: Cold oil: thick, high pressure initially, slow to warm. Hot oil: thin, low pressure, poor bearing film. Know your oil grade and acceptable pressure/temperature ranges. Monitor both gauges.
key_facts: Oil thickens when cold; thins when hot
Cold engine: high oil pressure, long warm-up needed
Hot engine: low oil pressure, risk of bearing damage
Oil viscosity must balance flow (thin) and bearing protection (thick)
SAE ratings (10, 30, 40, etc.) define oil weight
Multi-grade oils (e.g., 15W-50) adapt to temperature changes
Ashless dispersant oils maintain viscosity better than mineral oils
source: Wagtendonk Principles of Helicopter Flight, FAA Helicopter Handbook

syllabus_desc: Compare the features of the ashless dispersant (AD) oils used in piston aero- engines with straight mineral oil and detergent oils.
concept: Straight mineral oil is the simple stuff—refined petroleum with minimal additives. It's used for engine break-in because it lets piston rings and cylinder walls wear against each other to form a proper seal. Once break-in's done, you switch to ashless dispersant (AD) oil. AD oil contains additives that grab contamination particles (carbon, metal wear, acids) and hold them in suspension throughout the oil rather than letting them settle and form sludge. 'Ashless' means the additives don't leave metalite ash deposits when they burn—important because ash deposits on spark plugs and valve seats cause problems. Detergent oils (automotive type) clean existing deposits off surfaces, which sounds great except those loosened chunks can block oil passages and filters. That's why you never use automotive detergent oils in aircraft engines. AD oil prevents deposits forming in the first place without scrubbing existing ones loose. In your helicopter's piston engine, AD oil is the standard for normal operations after break-in. Don't mix mineral and AD oils unless the manufacturer specifically allows it.
exam_tip: Mineral oil = break-in only. AD oil = normal operations, holds contaminants in suspension, ashless (no ash deposits). Detergent oil = automotive, NOT for aircraft (loosens deposits that block passages). Never mix types unless approved.
key_facts: Straight mineral oil: minimal additives, used for break-in. Ashless dispersant (AD): holds contaminants in suspension, prevents sludge. 'Ashless' = no metallic ash deposits on plugs/valves. Detergent oils: automotive, loosens existing deposits (dangerous in aircraft). Never use automotive detergent oil in aero engines. Don't mix mineral and AD oils unless manufacturer-approved. AD oil is standard for normal operations post-break-in.
source: Wagtendonk Principles of Helicopter Flight, FAA Helicopter Handbook

syllabus_desc: With respect to oil grades, differentiate between the commercial aviation numbers and SAE ratings.
concept: Oil grades are specified two ways in aviation—commercial aviation numbers (developed specifically for aircraft) and SAE ratings (developed for automobiles and industries). The two systems roughly correspond but aren't identical, and you absolutely must follow your Pilot's Operating Handbook's specification exactly. Aviation oil grades use numbers like 80, 100, 120, and others. These numbers don't correspond directly to viscosity units; they're simply a numbering system developed by aviation authorities. The grade 100 is by far the most common for light piston aircraft and helicopters like the R22. SAE grades use a different numbering system: 10W, 20, 30, 40, 50, and so on. The 'W' stands for winter and applies to oils that flow at cold temperatures. SAE 20 is roughly equivalent to aviation 80, SAE 30 to aviation 100, and SAE 40 to aviation 120, but that's an approximation, not a precise conversion. If your POH says to use aviation 100, you should use aviation 100—not automobile SAE 30. The difference in additives and viscosity characteristics matters. Some modern aircraft and helicopters are approved for multi-viscosity oils (like SAE 15W-40), which flow better in cold conditions while maintaining proper viscosity when hot. But again, this must be explicitly approved in your POH. Read your operating handbook, stick to its specification, and don't assume equivalence between aviation and SAE grades.
exam_tip: Aviation grades (80, 100, 120) vs SAE (10W, 20, 30, 40); roughly correspond but not identical. Follow POH exactly, don't assume equivalence.
key_facts: Aviation grades: 80, 100, 120 developed for aircraft engines
SAE grades: 10W, 20, 30, 40, 50 for automotive and industrial
Roughly correspond: avi 80 ≈ SAE 20, avi 100 ≈ SAE 30, avi 120 ≈ SAE 40
NOT exact equivalents—additives and characteristics differ
Always use grade specified in POH, not assumed equivalent
W suffix in SAE grade indicates winter (cold flow) performance
Some modern engines approved for multi-viscosity (e.g. SAE 15W-40)
source: Wagtendonk Principles of Helicopter Flight, FAA Helicopter Handbook

syllabus_desc: Differentiate between a wet sump and a dry sump oil system.
concept: Wet sump and dry sump are two entirely different approaches to storing engine oil, and each has distinct advantages and limitations. A wet sump system is simpler and far more common in light aircraft and helicopters. The oil pan is bolted directly to the bottom of the engine block and serves as both the sump (storage reservoir) and the structural part of the engine. Oil gravity-feeds back into the pan after circulating through the engine. The system's simple, cheap to manufacture, reliable, and requires no additional plumbing beyond the engine itself. The downside is that a wet sump limits the aircraft's attitude envelope. The oil sump relies on gravity to keep oil against the pan's internal baffles and pickups. If you pitch the nose up steeply, roll inverted, or manoeuvre aggressively, the oil can slosh away from the pickup point, starving the oil pump and destroying the engine in seconds. Most light helicopters tolerate moderate attitudes, but extended inverted flight or extreme attitudes can cause oil starvation. That's why aerobatic aircraft avoid wet sumps. A dry sump system separates the oil storage tank from the engine itself. Oil circulates from the engine to a remote tank, and a scavenge pump actively sucks oil out of the engine and back to the tank. A separate feed pump delivers oil from the tank back into the engine. This system works in any attitude—aerobatic pilots love dry sumps. The downside is complexity: you've got multiple pumps, remote tank plumbing, and more components to maintain. It's also heavier due to the additional plumbing and tank. Most small piston helicopters use wet sump because of the weight and cost advantage, though some military or high-performance helicopters use dry sump.
exam_tip: Wet sump: pan bolted to engine, simple, attitude-limited. Dry sump: separate tank with scavenge pump, works any attitude, heavier/complex.
key_facts: Wet sump: oil pan integral to engine block, gravity return
Wet sump simple, cheap, reliable, but attitude-limited
Wet sump: oil can slosh away from pickup in steep attitudes
Dry sump: separate oil tank with scavenge and feed pumps
Dry sump works in any aircraft attitude including inverted
Dry sump: more complex, heavier, higher maintenance
Light helicopters typically use wet sump (weight/cost advantage)
source: Wagtendonk Principles of Helicopter Flight, FAA Helicopter Handbook

syllabus_desc: Describe the function of: (a) the engine-driven oil pump and pressure relief valve; (b) oil lines, passages and galleries; (c) oil sumps, and scavenge pumps; (d) oil cooler; (e) oil tank and filter.
concept: The engine-driven pump is a gear pump or rotary pump that's mechanically driven by the engine—as engine speed increases, pump output increases, delivering more oil pressure. The pressure relief valve prevents over-pressure: if pressure climbs too high (usually set around 60–80 psi), the relief opens and dumps excess oil back to the sump. Oil lines carry oil from the pump to galleries in the engine block. Galleries are internal passages casting through the engine, distributing oil to every bearing and critical surface. The sump is the oil pan at the engine bottom where used oil drains and collects for recirculation. In a dry sump system, a scavenge pump pulls used oil from the engine sump into a separate return tank. The oil cooler is a heat exchanger (like a small radiator) where oil passes through cooling fins and sheds heat to airflow or ram air. The oil tank holds the oil supply and provides residence time for contaminants to settle before recirculation. The filter traps debris—you'll change it at specified intervals (usually every 50–100 hours on a piston twin) and check it during pre-flight.
exam_tip: Pump: mechanical drive, delivers pressure. Relief: prevents over-pressure. Galleries: internal passages. Cooler: sheds heat. Filter: traps debris. Monitor pressure and temperature continuously.
key_facts: Engine-driven pump: gear or rotary, output increases with RPM
Pressure relief valve opens at ~60–80 psi
Oil galleries: internal passages distributing oil through engine block
Oil sump: collection pan at engine bottom
Scavenge pump (dry sump only): returns used oil to tank
Oil cooler: heat exchanger shedding heat to airflow
Oil filter: captures debris, replaced every 50–100 hours
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Explain the need for periodic oil changes.
concept: Oil degrades with use. The ashless dispersant additive gets depleted, the oil picks up moisture and contaminants, carbon buildup increases, and eventually the oil's no longer effective at lubrication and cooling. Typically, you'll change piston engine oil every 50–100 hours (your POH specifies exactly) or annually, whichever comes first. On a twin that's being flown regularly (50+ hours a month), you'll hit the hour limit first. If the aircraft is flown infrequently, oil degrades anyway and you'll hit the annual limit. The oil filter gets changed at every oil change—it's collecting debris, and once it's full of gunk, it restricts flow. Your pre-flight oil check is a visual inspection of quantity only; the contamination and degradation you can't see until you have oil analysed in a lab (which some operators do). Flying with old, degraded oil is like flying with a leaking seal bearing—you're running on borrowed time waiting for bearing failure.
exam_tip: Oil changes at 50–100 hour intervals or annually. Filter changed at every oil change. Old oil = poor lubrication and cooling. Stick to your POH change schedule.
key_facts: Oil degrades with time and use
Ashless dispersant additive depletes gradually
Typical change interval: 50–100 hours or annually
Whichever interval comes first is the limit
Oil filter changed at every oil change
Regular oil sampling can detect contamination early
Flying with expired oil risks bearing damage and engine failure
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Describe the likely results of operating an engine with: (a) incorrect oil type; (b) incorrect oil quantity.
concept: Using the wrong oil type—say, using automotive detergent oil or a different aviation grade than specified—causes several problems. Detergent oils leave ash residue in the combustion chambers, fouling plugs and reducing ignition efficiency. The additive package might not be compatible with avgas, causing sludge or varnish buildup. Wrong aviation grades (too thin or too thick) mean either poor bearing lubrication (thin oil) or clogged filters and pressure problems (thick oil). Operating with insufficient oil (low quantity) means less heat dissipation, higher oil temperature, lower pressure, and risk of bearing starvation. You'll see rising temps and dropping pressure on the gauges. Excess oil is also bad: it foams in the sump, reducing its viscosity, and the extra heat generated by friction and aeration will spike temperature. Your daily oil level check (cold, on a level surface) should show the oil within the green band on the dipstick. Too much or too little is a problem.
exam_tip: Wrong oil type: deposits, sludge, poor lubrication. Too little oil: high temp, low pressure, bearing risk. Too much oil: foaming, high temp. Always use specified grade and maintain level in green band.
key_facts: Wrong oil type causes deposits, sludge, additive incompatibility
Detergent (automotive) oils leave ash residue (not approved)
Wrong aviation grade: poor flow or poor lubrication
Low oil quantity: high temperature, low pressure, bearing starvation
Excess oil: foaming, increased friction, high temperature
Monitor oil level before each flight (cold, level surface)
Maintain level within green band on dipstick
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Describe the likely causes of: (a) low oil pressure; (b) high oil pressure; (c) high oil temperature.
concept: Low oil pressure mid-flight is usually a bearing wear issue: loose bearings have bigger gaps, oil leaks past instead of staying in the bearing film, pressure drops. It can also be a failed pressure relief valve (stuck open), a blocked oil filter restricting return flow, a faulty pump, or actual oil leakage from a line. High oil pressure is less common but usually means a stuck pressure relief valve (stays closed when it should open) or a clogged oil filter causing backpressure. High oil temperature usually comes from high oil load (big power settings, hot day, lots of electrical load or systems drawing power), inadequate cooling (fouled oil cooler, low airflow at low speed), or high friction from low oil quantity or viscosity too thick. Combining low pressure with high temperature is a red flag: it often means bearing wear—the bearing's loose, friction is high, oil's getting hot, pressure's dropping because the bearing isn't holding oil. That's a get-down-now situation. Your oil cooler can get fouled (dust, insects, salt spray if you're operating near the coast at places like Auckland), so check it visually during pre-flight.
exam_tip: Low pressure: bearing wear, pump failure, filter clog, relief stuck open. High pressure: relief stuck closed, filter clog. High temp: overload, poor cooling, low quantity. Low pressure + high temp = bearing wear = land urgently.
key_facts: Low oil pressure: bearing wear, pump failure, filter blockage, relief stuck open
High oil pressure: relief valve stuck closed, filter blockage
High oil temperature: high power setting, hot day, fouled cooler, insufficient quantity
Low pressure + high temperature: bearing wear (critical situation)
Monitor pressure and temperature continuously in flight
Oil cooler can foul from dust, insects, salt spray
Combination of low pressure and high temperature requires immediate landing
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Explain the relationship between a fluctuating or low oil pressure reading accompanied by a rise in oil temperature.
concept: This combination is a red flag that points to bearing wear. Here's the chain of events: as a bearing wears, the clearance between the rotating shaft and the bearing surface increases. Oil that should stay in a thin film between the surfaces starts leaking past, increasing oil flow but reducing the protective film. With less film pressure, the bearing surfaces make closer contact, friction rises, heat generation spikes. You see the oil temperature climbing. Meanwhile, the pressure reading drops because the worn bearing isn't holding pressure—the oil's just flowing through and leaking out. The fluctuation you're seeing is the pressure bouncing around as the bearing rocks or the oil flow becomes intermittent. This is a bearing on the way out. You're not going to recover from this in flight, and continuing to run the engine makes it worse. Declare a precautionary landing immediately and get the aircraft on the ground and into maintenance. Don't power back up until a mechanic has inspected and cleared it.
exam_tip: Low pressure + high temperature + fluctuation = bearing wear. Immediate landing required. Don't continue running. Each minute of flight accelerates bearing damage.
key_facts: Bearing wear increases clearance between shaft and bearing surface
Increased clearance = oil leakage from bearing film
Oil leakage = lower pressure, higher friction, higher temperature
Fluctuating pressure indicates bearing movement or intermittent contact
This combination is a critical failure in progress
Immediate landing required; do not continue flying
Aircraft grounded until maintenance inspection and repair
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Describe the actions the pilot should take in the low pressure/high temperature situation.
concept: You've got low pressure and high temperature gauges. First, verify it's a real problem and not a gauge fault: cross-check with the other engine if it's a twin, or look for other symptoms like roughness or unusual vibration. If both engines show normal readings, suspect the gauge. If it's one engine and real, reduce power immediately to the minimum needed to maintain safe flight and climb (if possible). Reducing load reduces friction and might bring the temperature down and pressure up slightly. Get a descent planned to your nearest suitable aerodrome—you're landing soon. Call ATC immediately and declare a precautionary landing; they'll clear vectors and prepare the field. Don't try to climb to altitude or stretch the glide; just head for the nearest place you can put it down safely. Avoid manoeuvres that increase engine load or G-forces. Once on the ground, ensure the engine is shut down properly and the aircraft is secured. Don't restart it; let maintenance inspect it. If you're at an outstation without immediate maintenance, inform the operator immediately.
exam_tip: Verify real (not gauge fault). Reduce power. Plan descent to nearest suitable airfield. Declare precautionary landing with ATC. No climbing or stretching. Land smoothly and shut down. Notify operator; don't restart.
key_facts: First: verify it's a real problem, not gauge failure
Reduce power immediately to minimum safe level
Plan descent to nearest suitable aerodrome
Declare precautionary landing with ATC early
Avoid manoeuvres increasing engine load
Land and shut down; do not restart
Secure aircraft and notify operator
Maintenance inspection required before further flight
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Describe: (a) mechanical advantage; (b) how mechanical advantage can be gained hydraulically; (c) the principle of operation of typical aircraft hydraulic services.
concept: Pascal's principle is elegant: pressure applied to a fluid in a confined space is transmitted equally in all directions. Squeeze a closed container of hydraulic fluid anywhere, and the pressure increase appears everywhere else. This is the entire foundation of hydraulic systems. You apply force to a small piston, pressure multiplies and transmits through the fluid, and comes out the other end to move whatever actuator you need—landing gear, flap jacks, trim systems. No compressibility in liquid means direct, responsive control.
exam_tip: Pressure in confined fluid = same everywhere. Simple statement, but it's the principle that makes hydraulics work. Know how this enables mechanical advantage.
key_facts: Pressure applied = pressure transmitted equally In all directions through fluid No loss or reduction in pressure Foundation of hydraulic systems
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the advantages of using hydraulics to operate aircraft services.
concept: Mechanical advantage means using a small force to move something heavy. Hydraulically, you achieve it with different-sized pistons. Say you apply force to a small input piston and it connects to a large output piston—the pressure's the same everywhere (Pascal), but force equals pressure times area. Small area input, large area output, and you've multiplied your force. Practical example: your landing gear extension might need thousands of pounds of force, but you get it from a small hydraulic pump that you can power with your engine. The pump doesn't need to be huge; it just needs to overcome pressure, which is modest. The area difference in cylinders does the heavy lifting.
exam_tip: Mechanical advantage = small force creates large force. Hydraulically: small input piston, large output piston. Know the formula: Force = Pressure × Area.
key_facts: Pressure same everywhere (Pascal) Force = Pressure × Area Small input piston, large output = force multiplication Hydraulic pump doesn't need high force, just modest pressure Different piston areas create advantage
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Differentiate between the three types of hydraulic oil.
concept: Your aircraft's hydraulic system starts with a pump driven by engine power, usually via a gearbox or belt. The pump forces hydraulic fluid through filter and pressure relief valve (prevents excessive pressure) into a distribution manifold. From there, fluid goes to various actuators—landing gear cylinders, flap jacks, trim units, brake systems. Each actuator is a cylinder with a piston; the fluid pressure moves the piston, doing work. Used fluid returns to a reservoir for reuse. Redundancy's built in: two independent systems, separate pumps, separate fluid supplies. If one system fails, the other keeps you flying. The entire system relies on incompressible fluid and sealed lines; any leak loses system pressure and function.
exam_tip: Pump → filter → relief valve → actuators → return reservoir. Redundancy is critical—know why. Be ready to explain what happens if you lose a hydraulic system.
key_facts: Engine-driven pump provides pressure Fluid flows to multiple actuators simultaneously Pressure relief prevents overpressure Dual systems for redundancy Return to reservoir for reuse Any leak = pressure loss = system failure
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, AIP NZ

syllabus_desc: Describe the function of common hydraulic system components including: (a) reservoirs; Advisory Circular AC61-5 Revision 38 Sub Topic Syllabus Item (b) pumps; (c) pressure regulators; (d) accumulators; (e) check valves and release valves; (f) selector valves; (g) actuators; (h) filters.
concept: The reservoir holds the bulk of the hydraulic fluid, provides a place for air bubbles to escape and contaminants to settle, and dissipates heat. Pumps are engine-driven or electric-driven and convert mechanical power into fluid pressure. Pressure regulators (relief valves) prevent over-pressure by opening if system pressure exceeds a set point (usually 2000–3000 psi), dumping excess flow back to the reservoir. Accumulators are like small pressure vessels that store pressurised fluid—they smooth out pressure spikes, provide emergency power if the pump fails, and absorb shock. Check valves allow flow in one direction only, preventing backflow. Selector (directional control) valves route hydraulic flow to different actuators depending on the pilot's input—left, right, up, down, etc. Release valves (not to be confused with relief valves) are manually operated and allow emergency decompression or manual lowering of systems if hydraulic power is lost. Actuators are the working end: hydraulic cylinders that extend and retract (landing gear, flaps, trim) or rotary actuators that rotate (flight controls). Filters catch contaminants to protect the system and maintain fluid cleanliness.
exam_tip: Each component has a job: reservoir holds fluid, pump pressurises it, regulator prevents over-pressure, accumulator provides smoothing and backup, check valves prevent backflow, selectors route flow, actuators do the work, filters keep it clean.
key_facts: Reservoir: stores fluid, allows air escape, dissipates heat
Pump: converts mechanical power to hydraulic pressure
Pressure regulator (relief): opens if pressure exceeds set point
Accumulator: stores pressure, smooths spikes, provides emergency power
Check valve: allows one-way flow, prevents backflow
Selector valve: directs flow to desired actuator
Release valve: allows manual decompression or emergency lowering
Actuator: hydraulic cylinder or rotary motor that performs work
Filter: removes contaminants, maintains fluid cleanliness
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Describe the operation of the following hydraulic systems: (a) open centre system; (b) pressurised system.
concept: An open centre system is simple: hydraulic fluid is always flowing, circulating from the pump through various selector valves and back to the reservoir, even when no actuators are being commanded. When you want to operate an actuator (say, lower the landing gear), you move the selector valve which directs the flow into the actuator cylinder, pushing it. Excess flow goes back to the reservoir. It's straightforward, no complex regulators, but it's wasteful—the pump is always spinning and generating heat. Pressurised (or closed centre) systems are more efficient: the pump is on demand. Fluid sits idle in the accumulators until the pilot commands an actuator. Moving the selector valve directs pressurised fluid to the actuator and simultaneously opens the pump (or starts it) if pressure has dropped below a threshold. The pump runs only when needed, reducing heat and improving efficiency. Pressurised systems have pressure regulators and accumulators to maintain a reserve of pressurised fluid. Most modern aircraft use pressurised systems because they're efficient and reliable. Open centre systems are lighter and simpler, so you'll find them on some older or very light aircraft.
exam_tip: Open centre: pump always runs, fluid always flowing, simple but inefficient. Pressurised: pump on demand, accumulator keeps pressure ready, efficient. Know which your aircraft uses and how to operate it.
key_facts: Open centre: pump always running, constant flow, high heat generation
Pressurised (closed centre): pump on demand, accumulators store pressure, efficient
Open centre: simpler, lighter, less maintenance
Pressurised: more complex, heavier, more efficient
Pressurised systems require regulators and accumulators
Modern aircraft typically use pressurised systems
Selector valve operation differs between system types
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge
