syllabus_desc: Explain the causes of dragging in a fully articulated rotor system.
concept: Dragging (also called lead-lag) in a fully articulated rotor system happens because of conservation of angular momentum—the same principle that makes an ice skater spin faster when they pull their arms in. As a rotor blade flaps upward, its centre of mass moves closer to the rotor shaft (the axis of rotation). To conserve angular momentum, the blade speeds up slightly—it 'leads' ahead of its normal position. When the blade flaps down, its centre of mass moves further from the shaft, it slows down, and 'lags' behind. This constant leading and lagging with each revolution would tear the blade roots apart without a drag hinge (lead-lag hinge) to accommodate the movement. Drag dampers (hydraulic or elastomeric) are fitted to control the rate of this movement and prevent a dangerous oscillation called ground resonance—where the lead-lag movement of the blades synchronises with the natural frequency of the landing gear, building into violent oscillation that can destroy the helicopter in seconds.
exam_tip: Dragging/lead-lag caused by conservation of angular momentum during flapping. Blade up = closer to shaft = speeds up (leads). Blade down = further from shaft = slows (lags). Drag hinges accommodate this. Drag dampers prevent ground resonance.
key_facts: Dragging (lead-lag): caused by conservation of angular momentum. Blade flaps up → centre of mass closer to shaft → blade speeds up (leads). Blade flaps down → centre of mass further from shaft → blade slows (lags). Drag hinge accommodates this cyclic movement. Drag dampers control rate of lead-lag movement. Without dampers: risk of ground resonance. Ground resonance: lead-lag synchronises with landing gear frequency. Can destroy helicopter in seconds.
source: Wagtendonk Principles of Helicopter Flight, FAA Helicopter Handbook

syllabus_desc: Explain the function of the blade damper in fully articulated rotor systems.
concept: The rotor brake is a mechanical friction brake that stops the main rotor spin-down after engine shutdown, reducing the time to full stop from several minutes to under 20 seconds. This is important for operational efficiency and safety—you don't want a spinning rotor disc overhead while you're working on the ground. The brake is typically a friction disc mechanism that's engaged by a switch or is automatically engaged when the engine shuts down. In modern helicopters, rotor speed might be controlled by a governor that automatically adjusts engine throttle to maintain constant rotor RPM, giving the pilot less workload. Autorotation is the helicopter's emergency descent mode—if the engine fails, the rotor blades continue spinning from the upward airflow during descent, allowing a controlled landing from any altitude above minimum. This self-regulating aerodynamic principle is what makes helicopter single-engine-failure survival possible. Most helicopter accidents involve failure to recognise an engine failure quickly enough to enter autorotation.
exam_tip: Rotor brake stops rotor after shutdown. Autorotation is survival mode on engine failure—upward air keeps rotor turning. Know that you must maintain rotor RPM in limits during autorotation.
key_facts: Rotor brake speeds rotor shutdown to ~20 seconds
Friction disc mechanism
Autorotation uses upward air flow to keep rotor turning
Governor systems maintain rotor RPM automatically

syllabus_desc: Explain the reason a semi-rigid (two-bladed) rotor system is frequently under- slung.
concept: A semi-rigid (teetering) rotor is frequently underslung—meaning the rotor blades are attached below the teetering hinge rather than at it or above it. The reason is to reduce flapping-induced vibration. When a teetering rotor flaps, the rising blade's tip traces a slightly higher arc, and the descending blade's tip traces a lower arc. If the blades were attached at the hinge point, this teetering would cause the blade tips to move in and out (toward and away from the mast) with each revolution—a sort of lateral 'hunting' motion called Coriolis effect, similar to the lead-lag in articulated systems. By undersinging the rotor (lowering the attachment point), the blade's centre of mass stays closer to the same plane during teetering, minimising this lateral movement. The result is less vibration transmitted to the mast and airframe. It's a clever geometric trick. The R22 and R44 both use underslung semi-rigid rotors—if you look at the rotor head, you can see the blades sit below the teetering bolt, not at it.
exam_tip: Underslung = blades attached below teetering hinge. Reduces Coriolis effect (lateral hunting) during flapping. Less vibration transmitted to mast. R22 and R44 use underslung semi-rigid rotors.
key_facts: Underslung: blade attachment point below the teetering hinge. Reduces Coriolis effect during teetering. Minimises lateral blade hunting (in/out movement). Less vibration transmitted to mast and airframe. Geometric solution to flapping-induced vibration. R22 and R44 use underslung semi-rigid rotors. Blade centre of mass stays closer to same plane during flapping.
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the design feature (advance angle) which compensates for phase lag.
concept: Phase lag in a rotor system means that the maximum blade displacement occurs approximately 90° after the point where the maximum aerodynamic input was applied. To compensate, the advance angle (or phase angle) offsets the control input point ahead of the desired effect point. In practical terms, if you want the rotor disc to tilt forward, the blade pitch change must be applied 90° before the forward position. The swashplate is rigged with this advance angle built in, so when the pilot pushes the cyclic forward, the pitch change actually occurs at the correct point in rotation to produce a forward tilt. Without this compensation, cyclic inputs would produce disc tilt 90° away from the intended direction.
exam_tip: Phase lag = 90° delay between input and response. Advance angle compensates by applying pitch change 90° early. Built into swashplate rigging.
key_facts: Phase lag: max displacement 90° after max input
Advance angle offsets control input 90° ahead
Built into swashplate rigging
Without it, disc tilts 90° from intended direction
Applies to all cyclic control inputs
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, Principles of Helicopter Flight (Wagtendonk)

syllabus_desc: Explain the reason for rigging the rotor mast away from the helicopter vertical axis.
concept: The mast isn't perfectly vertical—it's rigged slightly away from vertical to compensate for tail rotor drift and other aerodynamic effects. This angle's called rigging or pre-tilt. Get it wrong and the rotor disc tilts, causing control problems and inefficiency. If you're flying an R22 and notice the cyclic needs to be offset to maintain level flight, the mast alignment might be off and it's a maintenance check. The angle is typically just a couple of degrees, but it makes a difference in how the helicopter responds and where the CG needs to be.
exam_tip: Understand mast rigging affects rotor disc tilt and control feel.
key_facts: Mast rigged away from vertical to compensate for tail rotor drift
Incorrect rigging causes cyclic offset and control inefficiency
Rigging angle typically 2–3 degrees from vertical
Affects rotor disc tilt and natural trim position
source: Wagtendonk Principles of Helicopter Flight, FAA Helicopter Handbook

syllabus_desc: Describe the methods of chordwise and spanwise balancing.
concept: Spanwise balancing ensures each blade section from root to tip has even weight distribution along its length. Chordwise balancing addresses fore-aft weight distribution (leading edge vs trailing edge). Both are critical. If the spanwise balance is off, one half of the rotor disc is heavier, and you get a 1-per-rev vibration that shakes the whole helicopter. Chordwise imbalance causes a different vibration signature. Balancing weights glued to the blade or inboard sections of the blade correct these issues. Modern helicopters use precision methods; older aircraft might need more frequent rebalancing.
exam_tip: Understand both types of imbalance cause vibration, but different kinds.
key_facts: Spanwise imbalance: uneven weight along blade length, 1-per-rev vibration
Chordwise imbalance: uneven fore-aft weight distribution
Balance weights glued to blade correct imbalance
Testing done with rotor at flight RPM using accelerometers
source: Wagtendonk Principles of Helicopter Flight, FAA Helicopter Handbook

syllabus_desc: Explain the reason for sweepback design near main rotor blade tips.
concept: Blade attachment is where all the lifting force concentrates. You've got a root section that bolts or is welded to the hub. Hinges—flapping hinges, lead-lag hinges, feathering hinges—allow controlled movement. The attachment design has to handle immense aerodynamic forces, centrifugal loads (especially at the tip), and bending moments. Material choice is critical—stainless steel hinges, composite-wrapped roots, titanium fasteners. Engineers design these to flex and move with the aerodynamic loads while staying rock-solid. If you see any cracking around a hinge during inspection, it's a grounding item.
exam_tip: Understand why blade attachments are inspected so carefully. They're critical load paths.
key_facts: Root attachment transfers all aerodynamic and centrifugal forces
Flapping, lead-lag, and feathering hinges allow controlled blade movement
Materials chosen for fatigue resistance and strength (titanium, stainless steel)
Inspection of attachment areas critical; cracks are unairworthy
source: Wagtendonk Principles of Helicopter Flight, FAA Helicopter Handbook

syllabus_desc: Explain the reason for washout in the design of main rotor blades.
concept: Every design feature on a rotor has a purpose. Some reduce vibration, some improve aerodynamic efficiency, some improve control authority. Features like blade twist (washout), blade sweep, and stabiliser bars all work together to manage the rotor's response to pilot input and environmental loads. Some features like delta-three hinges couple flapping to pitch—when a blade flaps up, it automatically increases pitch slightly, reducing flapping amplitude. Understanding why these features exist helps you understand how your helicopter responds.
exam_tip: Don't just memorise features—understand the 'why'. Questions test your reasoning.
key_facts: Washout (blade twist) distributes lift along span evenly
Blade sweep delays compressibility effects at advancing tip
Delta-three hinge couples flapping to pitch change, reduces amplitude
Stabiliser bar damps rotor flapping, improves control stability
source: Wagtendonk Principles of Helicopter Flight, FAA Helicopter Handbook

syllabus_desc: Explain the advantages of employing a delta-three hinge in a fully articulated rotor system.
concept: A delta-three hinge (also written δ3) is a clever geometric arrangement in a fully articulated rotor system where the flapping hinge is angled rather than perpendicular to the blade span. This angle means that when a blade flaps up, the hinge geometry automatically reduces its pitch (angle of attack), and when it flaps down, pitch increases. Why's that useful? It provides automatic pitch-flap coupling that reduces blade flapping amplitude. Without it, a blade encountering a gust that causes it to flap up would maintain its pitch, generate even more lift, and flap further up. With delta-three, the upward flap reduces pitch, reducing lift, and self-limiting the flap. It's a natural damping mechanism. This is especially valuable on tail rotors, where excessive flapping can bring blades dangerously close to the tail boom. Many tail rotors use a significant delta-three angle for exactly this reason. On main rotors of fully articulated systems, the delta-three effect provides smoother flapping response and reduced vibration without adding mechanical complexity—it's achieved purely through hinge geometry.
exam_tip: Delta-three hinge: angled flapping hinge. Blade flaps up → pitch reduces. Blade flaps down → pitch increases. Provides automatic pitch-flap coupling. Reduces flapping amplitude. Especially important on tail rotors (boom clearance).
key_facts: Delta-three (δ3) hinge: flapping hinge angled rather than perpendicular. Blade flaps up → automatic pitch decrease. Blade flaps down → automatic pitch increase. Provides pitch-flap coupling. Self-limiting flap response (natural damping). Reduces flapping amplitude. Especially valuable on tail rotors for boom clearance. Achieved through hinge geometry alone—no added complexity. Smoother flapping response and reduced vibration.
source: Wagtendonk Principles of Helicopter Flight, FAA Helicopter Handbook

syllabus_desc: Explain the purpose of employing an offset pitch horn in a rotor system.
concept: The pitch horn (the attachment point where pitch links connect to the blade) is offset from the flapping hinge axis. When a blade flaps, this offset geometry automatically changes the blade pitch. It's similar to delta-three but integrated differently in fully articulated systems. The offset reduces flapping amplitude and couples pitch control to blade motion. This coupling improves stability and control authority. Designers fine-tune the offset angle to get the desired control feel—too much and control gets sensitive, too little and the helicopter feels sluggish.
exam_tip: Offset pitch horn is an alternative to delta-three, used in articulated systems.
key_facts: Pitch horn offset from flapping hinge causes automatic pitch change with flap
Reduces flapping amplitude and improves control feel
Used in fully articulated rotor systems
Tuned offset angle affects control sensitivity and stability
source: Wagtendonk Principles of Helicopter Flight, FAA Helicopter Handbook

syllabus_desc: Explain the normal methods of trimming controls in a helicopter.
concept: Autorotation is the helicopter's equivalent of a fixed-wing glide—when the engine fails, the rotor blades continue spinning using the aerodynamic forces of air flowing upward through them. Here's the physics: as the helicopter descends from engine failure, air flows upward through the rotor disc. This upward airflow creates lift on the blades, and critically, creates a force component that drives the rotor around. The lift is greatest near the tips and outer portions of the blade (where airflow velocity is higher), and this creates a net torque that keeps the rotor spinning. By manipulating collective pitch, you can control descent rate and rotor RPM. You descend at about 1,500–2,000 feet per minute (depending on weight and blade design), maintain rotor RPM in the allowable range, and land. Most helicopters can autorotate successfully from any altitude if height and rotor RPM are within limits. This is drilled extensively in training—autorotation from height is a critical emergency procedure that's regularly practised to landing.
exam_tip: Autorotation = rotor driven by upward air flow when engine fails. Descent rate about 1,500 ft/min. Must maintain rotor RPM in limits. Know this is trainable and survivable.
key_facts: Rotor driven by upward air flow after engine failure
Lift on blades creates torque that drives rotation
Descent rate ~1,500 ft/min (depending on weight)
Requires discipline with collective pitch control

syllabus_desc: Describe the various methods of rotor stabilisation.
concept: Rotor stabilisation methods prevent unwanted oscillations and help the rotor maintain a steady disc attitude. The Bell stabiliser bar (flybar) is a weighted bar mounted at 90 degrees to the rotor blades on semi-rigid systems. Gyroscopic rigidity keeps the bar steady in space, and linkages from the bar to the blade pitch change mechanisms resist rapid changes in disc attitude—it acts as a mechanical damper, smoothing out gusts and control inputs. The R22 uses a stabiliser bar. The Hiller control system uses small auxiliary aerofoils (paddles) on a control bar. Pilot cyclic input tilts the paddles, which aerodynamically tilt the control bar, which then changes main blade pitch. This provides a mechanical advantage and smoother control response. Some systems use both (Bell-Hiller mixer). Modern helicopters increasingly use electronic stabilisation—FADEC systems and stability augmentation systems (SAS) that use gyros and accelerometers to detect unwanted motion and automatically apply corrective control inputs through hydraulic actuators. Offset flapping hinges on articulated systems also provide a stabilising effect by creating a hub moment that resists disc tilt.
exam_tip: Stabilisation methods: Bell stabiliser bar (flybar, gyroscopic), Hiller paddles (aerodynamic), electronic SAS, offset flapping hinges. Know how each works. R22 = stabiliser bar. Modern turbine types = electronic SAS.
key_facts: Bell stabiliser bar (flybar): weighted bar at 90° to blades, gyroscopic rigidity resists disc tilt. Hiller system: auxiliary aerofoil paddles, aerodynamic control. Bell-Hiller mixer: combines both approaches. Electronic SAS: gyros and accelerometers detect motion, hydraulic actuators correct. FADEC integration for automatic stabilisation. Offset flapping hinges: create hub moments that resist disc tilt. R22 uses stabiliser bar. Modern helicopters increasingly use electronic systems.
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Describe the susceptibility of the various rotor systems to ground resonance.
concept: Ground resonance is a nasty phenomenon that happens with articulated rotors when you're on the ground. The blades go out of phase in their lead-lag motion (one blade leads forward, the adjacent one lags behind), and this imbalance rocks the fuselage. The fuselage bounces, which changes blade pitch and load, which pushes the resonance harder. The helicopter can literally bounce itself to pieces on the ground. It happens without warning and accelerates violently. The fix is immediate: either get the wheels off the ground (fly away) or shut down immediately. You never get caught between those options—if the rotor starts acting weird on the ground, power up and get airborne or cut power and secure the aircraft. Prevention: good tracking, balanced blades, smooth rotor engagement.
exam_tip: Ground resonance is catastrophic. Know the answer: fly away or land immediately.
key_facts: Out-of-phase blade lead-lag motion causes fuselage resonance on ground
Fuselage bouncing couples back into blade motion, self-amplifying
Can result in structural failure if not arrested immediately
Recovery: immediate lift-off or immediate shutdown, no in-between
source: Wagtendonk Principles of Helicopter Flight, FAA Helicopter Handbook

syllabus_desc: Describe the susceptibility of the various rotor systems to mast bumping.
concept: Mast bumping is an even worse scenario and it's unique to semi-rigid rotors like the R22. The rotor teeter hinge has limits. If you push the nose down hard (forward cyclic) while in a descent and negative G loads develop, the rotor disc cones up and the blade roots actually contact the mast. The moment they touch, there's catastrophic structural damage—the mast cracks, the blades can fail, and you're uncontrollable. It happens in seconds. The prevention is simple: never push forward cyclic aggressively, never allow negative G (pull the stick when you need forward motion, don't push), and avoid diving the aircraft. If you're in a descent and need to recover, pull aft cyclic gently. Mast bumping is the reason R22 pilots respect their aircraft and fly with discipline.
exam_tip: Mast bump happens with abrupt forward cyclic + negative G. Prevention: maintain positive G.
key_facts: Semi-rigid rotor teeter hinge has physical limits
Forward cyclic + negative G causes rotor cone to contact mast
Mast contact causes instant structural failure
Prevention: smooth control inputs, maintain positive G loads always
source: Wagtendonk Principles of Helicopter Flight, FAA Helicopter Handbook

syllabus_desc: Describe the various types of vibration which may occur in a helicopter, their causes and possible remedies.
concept: Helicopter vibrations come in several flavours, each telling you something different. Low-frequency vibrations (1-per-rev or 2-per-rev) relate to the main rotor—one bump per blade per revolution. Causes include out-of-track blades (one blade flying higher than the other), out-of-balance blades (unequal weight), bent pitch links, or worn bearings. You feel these as a lateral shake or vertical bounce at rotor speed. Medium-frequency vibrations come from tail rotor issues or drive shaft problems—a faster buzz than main rotor vibration. High-frequency vibrations originate from the transmission gears and bearings—felt as a tingling or buzz through the airframe. Remedies depend on the source: main rotor tracking and balancing for low-frequency, tail rotor balancing and drive shaft inspection for medium, and transmission/bearing inspection for high-frequency. Ground resonance is a special case—a violent oscillation where main rotor lead-lag and landing gear frequency synchronise. The fix is immediate: if on the ground with RPM, lift off or shut down immediately. If RPM is insufficient to fly, reduce throttle to idle and apply rotor brake.
exam_tip: Low-frequency = main rotor (tracking, balance). Medium = tail rotor/drive shaft. High = transmission/gears. Ground resonance = special case, immediate action required. Know causes and remedies for each.
key_facts: Low-frequency (1/2-per-rev): main rotor—out-of-track, imbalance, bent links, worn bearings. Felt as lateral shake or vertical bounce. Medium-frequency: tail rotor, drive shaft problems. Felt as faster buzz. High-frequency: transmission gears and bearings. Felt as tingling/buzz. Ground resonance: lead-lag synchronises with landing gear frequency. Ground resonance response: lift off if RPM sufficient, or shut down and apply rotor brake. Remedies: tracking/balancing, drive shaft inspection, transmission inspection.
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Describe the design feature employed to reduce vibration in semi-rigid rotor systems.
concept: The primary design feature to reduce vibration in semi-rigid rotor systems is the underslung rotor head. By mounting the blades below the teetering hinge point, the Coriolis effect (lateral blade hunting during teetering) is minimised, which dramatically reduces the vibration transmitted into the mast and airframe. Additionally, the stabiliser bar (flybar) fitted to many semi-rigid systems acts as a vibration damper by resisting rapid changes in disc attitude through gyroscopic rigidity. Some semi-rigid systems also use elastomeric bearings at the teetering hinge—these rubber-like bearings absorb vibration energy that would otherwise travel into the mast. Blade design plays a role too: swept tips and tapered planforms reduce aerodynamic vibration sources. And vibration-absorbing mounts between the transmission and airframe (like rubber bushings or tuned vibration absorbers) isolate whatever vibration the rotor does produce from the cabin. The combination of underslung geometry, stabiliser bar, elastomeric bearings, and isolation mounts gives semi-rigid systems a smoother ride than their simple two-blade design might suggest.
exam_tip: Key vibration reduction in semi-rigid: underslung rotor (reduces Coriolis), stabiliser bar (gyroscopic damping), elastomeric bearings, vibration-absorbing mounts. Underslung is the primary design feature.
key_facts: Primary feature: underslung rotor head—reduces Coriolis effect. Stabiliser bar: gyroscopic rigidity damps rapid disc changes. Elastomeric bearings at teetering hinge absorb vibration. Vibration-absorbing mounts between transmission and airframe. Swept tips and tapered blade planforms reduce aerodynamic vibration. Combination provides smoother ride than expected from two-blade system.
source: Wagtendonk Principles of Helicopter Flight, FAA Helicopter Handbook

syllabus_desc: Describe the construction of modern tail rotors and their hubs.
concept: Modern tail rotors are typically two or four-blade assemblies mounted on a hub at the end of the tail boom. The blades are usually composite construction—fibreglass or carbon fibre over a foam or honeycomb core—for strength, light weight, and damage tolerance. Two-blade tail rotors are common on lighter helicopters (R22 has a two-blade tail rotor). Larger machines often use four blades for smoother operation and lower noise. The hub connects the blades to the tail rotor gearbox output shaft and includes the pitch change mechanism. On fully articulated tail rotor hubs, each blade has a feathering bearing allowing pitch change. Semi-rigid or teetering hubs (like on the R22) use a simpler arrangement. The pitch change is driven by the tail rotor pedals through a system of push-pull rods or cables running inside the tail boom, ending at a pitch change spider or crosshead at the hub. Delta-three hinge geometry is commonly used to provide pitch-flap coupling that limits tail rotor flapping—keeping blades clear of the tail boom. The tail rotor disc plane is typically mounted vertically, at the top or side of the vertical fin.
exam_tip: Modern tail rotors: composite blades (fibreglass/carbon fibre), two or four blades. Hub types: fully articulated or semi-rigid. Pitch change: via push-pull rods/cables from pedals. Delta-three hinge geometry common. Mounted vertically on tail fin.
key_facts: Tail rotor: two or four blades at end of tail boom. Composite construction: fibreglass or carbon fibre over foam/honeycomb core. Hub connects blades to tail rotor gearbox output shaft. Pitch change mechanism at hub: feathering bearings. Pitch control: push-pull rods or cables from pedals through tail boom. Pitch change spider/crosshead at hub. Delta-three hinge geometry for pitch-flap coupling. Limits flapping for boom clearance. Mounted vertically on or near vertical fin.
source: Wagtendonk Principles of Helicopter Flight, FAA Helicopter Handbook

syllabus_desc: Explain the purpose of a strike tab on the tail rotor blades.
concept: The tail rotor's close to the ground, especially during low-hover manoeuvres. A strike tab is a small, expendable section on the blade tip—it's designed to hit the ground first if you're too low. The tab absorbs the impact, and you might bend or crack the tab, but the main blade structure survives. Without it, you'd break a blade on the ground, which could fail suddenly in flight. The strike tab is a small cost for a potentially catastrophic failure avoidance. After any tail rotor strike, the tab and potentially the blade need inspection and replacement. It's a clear signal to the pilot: you were too low.
exam_tip: Strike tab protects blade tip. A struck tab needs inspection and likely replacement.
key_facts: Strike tab is expendable blade section designed for ground contact
Absorbs impact energy, protecting main blade
Requires inspection and replacement after any strike
Indicates pilot error: improper altitude awareness during low hover

syllabus_desc: Describe the: (a) advantages associated with conventional exposed tail rotors. (b) disadvantages associated with conventional exposed tail rotors. Advisory Circular AC61-5 Revision 38 Sub Topic Syllabus Item
concept: Conventional exposed tail rotors have several advantages: they're mechanically simple, well-understood technology with decades of service history. They're efficient at producing anti-torque thrust—the open disc can move a lot of air. Maintenance is straightforward because everything's accessible. Autorotation characteristics are predictable. And they provide direct, responsive yaw control. The disadvantages are significant though. Exposed blades are a safety hazard to ground personnel—tail rotor strikes are a real risk, especially during confined area operations or when people approach the helicopter from the wrong direction. They're vulnerable to foreign object damage (rocks, debris kicked up during landing). They're noisy—the tail rotor blade tips often go supersonic, generating a distinctive high-pitched whine. Ground clearance can be marginal, especially on uneven surfaces or during slope landings. And they consume engine power—typically 10-15% of total engine power just to counteract main rotor torque, power that's unavailable for lift. In windy conditions, tail rotor effectiveness can vary significantly, particularly with quartering tailwinds.
exam_tip: Advantages: simple, efficient, accessible, responsive yaw control. Disadvantages: ground personnel safety hazard, FOD vulnerable, noisy, power consumption (10-15%), limited ground clearance, affected by quartering tailwinds.
key_facts: Advantages: mechanically simple, well-proven technology. Efficient anti-torque thrust production. Accessible for maintenance. Predictable autorotation characteristics. Direct, responsive yaw control. Disadvantages: safety hazard to ground personnel. Vulnerable to foreign object damage. Noisy (supersonic blade tips). Consumes 10-15% of engine power. Marginal ground clearance on uneven surfaces. Affected by quartering tailwinds.
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Describe the: — (a) advantages associated with conventional exposed tail rotors
key_facts: [PENDING]

syllabus_desc: Describe the: — (b) disadvantages associated with conventional exposed tail rotors. Advisory Circular AC61-5 Revision 38 Sub Topic Syllabus Item
key_facts: [PENDING]

syllabus_desc: Explain the: (a) advantages associated with shrouded (Fenestron) tail rotors. (b) disadvantages associated with shrouded tail rotors.
concept: A shrouded tail rotor (Fenestron) encloses multiple small blades inside a duct or shroud built into the vertical fin. Advantages: much safer for ground personnel since the blades are hidden inside the shroud—no exposed spinning disc. Quieter than conventional tail rotors because the shroud contains noise and prevents blade tip vortices. Better protection against FOD since debris can't easily reach the blades. Ground clearance is built in because the shroud is integrated into the fin structure. And the shroud acts as an aerodynamic duct, increasing efficiency at certain flight conditions. Disadvantages: the Fenestron is heavier than a conventional tail rotor of equivalent thrust—the shroud structure adds weight. It's mechanically more complex with more blades and a more complicated hub. Maintenance access is limited because the blades are inside the shroud. It's less efficient in hover (requires more power than an equivalent conventional tail rotor at low speeds). And directional control can be less responsive at very low speeds compared to a conventional tail rotor. The Fenestron is used on aircraft like the Eurocopter EC130 and EC135.
exam_tip: Fenestron advantages: safer (enclosed), quieter, FOD protected, good ground clearance, ducted efficiency. Disadvantages: heavier, complex, limited maintenance access, less efficient in hover, less responsive at low speed.
key_facts: Fenestron: shrouded multi-blade tail rotor in duct. Advantages: safer for ground personnel (enclosed blades). Quieter (shroud contains noise, no tip vortices). Better FOD protection. Integrated ground clearance. Ducted efficiency at certain conditions. Disadvantages: heavier than conventional (shroud weight). Mechanically more complex. Limited maintenance access. Less efficient in hover. Less responsive at very low speeds. Used on: EC130, EC135.
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the: — (a) advantages associated with shrouded (Fenestron) tail rotors
key_facts: [PENDING]

syllabus_desc: Explain the: — (b) disadvantages associated with shrouded tail rotors
key_facts: [PENDING]

syllabus_desc: Explain the reason asymmetric aerofoils tend to be employed on tail rotors and not on main rotors.
concept: Tail rotor blades use asymmetric aerofoils (non-symmetrical cross-sections)—one surface is curved, the other flat or less curved. This design generates thrust more efficiently in one direction. The pitch control inputs change the blade angle to generate thrust forward or aft (in the rotor plane). Without asymmetric aerofoils, you'd need more blade area and higher RPM to get the same anti-torque force. The asymmetry also means there's a preferred direction; you get more efficient yaw authority in one direction. The R22's tail rotor is an excellent example of this efficiency.
exam_tip: Asymmetric aerofoils = efficient yaw control. Remember the concept.
key_facts: Asymmetric (non-symmetrical) blade cross-section
One surface curved, other flat or less curved
Generates thrust more efficiently in primary direction
Pitch control varies blade angle for forward and aft yaw thrust

syllabus_desc: Describe alternate methods of anti-torque control.
concept: Beyond conventional and Fenestron tail rotors, there are other ways to counteract main rotor torque. NOTAR (No Tail Rotor) uses a fan inside the tail boom that blows air through slots along the boom's right side. This air, combined with the main rotor downwash, creates a Coanda effect—the airflow 'sticks' to the curved boom surface and generates a sideways force that counters torque. A controllable jet thruster at the tail provides additional yaw control. NOTAR is very quiet and eliminates the tail rotor strike hazard entirely. The MD 520N and MD 902 use this system. Tandem rotor helicopters (like the Chinook) use two main rotors spinning in opposite directions—torque from each cancels the other, eliminating the need for any anti-torque device. Coaxial rotors (like the Kamov Ka-32) stack two rotors on the same mast, spinning in opposite directions. Same principle: counter-rotating rotors cancel torque. Tip jets—where thrust at the blade tips drives the rotor—produce no torque reaction on the fuselage at all, but are rare and inefficient. Each approach has trade-offs in complexity, weight, efficiency, and noise.
exam_tip: NOTAR: fan + Coanda effect on boom + jet thruster. No tail rotor hazard, quiet. Tandem rotors: two main rotors, opposite rotation (Chinook). Coaxial: stacked counter-rotating rotors (Kamov). Tip jets: no torque reaction. Know the principle behind each.
key_facts: NOTAR: fan in tail boom, air through slots, Coanda effect on boom surface + jet thruster. Very quiet, no tail rotor hazard. Used on MD 520N, MD 902. Tandem rotors: two main rotors, opposite rotation, torque cancelled. Chinook uses tandem. Coaxial rotors: two rotors on same mast, counter-rotating. Kamov Ka-32 uses coaxial. Tip jets: thrust at blade tips, no torque reaction on fuselage. Rare and inefficient.
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Describe the design features which can be employed to reduce tail rotor roll.
concept: Tail rotor roll is the tendency for the helicopter to roll in the direction of the tail rotor thrust. Because the tail rotor produces a sideways force (to counteract torque), it also pushes the tail sideways—and since the tail rotor is mounted above or to the side of the aircraft's centre of gravity, this force creates a rolling moment. In hover, you'll notice the helicopter hangs slightly skid-low on one side. Design features to reduce this include: canting the tail rotor slightly (angling the tail rotor disc to direct some of its thrust vertically, reducing the horizontal rolling component), tilting the main rotor mast slightly to one side so the main rotor's lift vector counteracts the tail rotor roll, mounting the tail rotor on the opposite side of the fin to change the rolling geometry, and designing the vertical fin to produce a sideways aerodynamic force in forward flight that offsets the tail rotor thrust. The R22, for example, has a slight main mast tilt to compensate. In forward flight, the vertical fin generates a side force that partially relieves the tail rotor's anti-torque burden, reducing the rolling tendency.
exam_tip: Tail rotor roll: sideways force creates rolling moment. Reduced by: canted tail rotor, tilted main mast, tail rotor mounting position, vertical fin aerodynamics in forward flight. Know why helicopter hovers slightly skid-low.
key_facts: Tail rotor roll: sideways anti-torque thrust creates rolling moment. Helicopter hangs slightly skid-low in hover. Design features to reduce: Canted tail rotor (angled to direct some thrust vertically). Tilted main rotor mast (lift vector counteracts roll). Tail rotor mounting position on fin. Vertical fin produces side force in forward flight. R22: slight mast tilt compensates. Forward flight: fin offloads tail rotor, reducing roll tendency.
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Describe the: (a) purpose of the collective control; (b) principle of the operation of the collective control.
concept: The collective control is the lever beside your left seat (or between seats in most training helicopters). Its purpose is to change the pitch angle of all main rotor blades simultaneously—collectively—to increase or decrease total rotor thrust. Pull it up, all blades increase pitch equally, more lift. Push it down, less pitch, less lift. The principle of operation: the collective lever connects mechanically (through pushrods and bellcranks) to the swashplate. Raising the collective lifts the entire swashplate assembly vertically along the mast axis. Since the rotating upper swashplate is connected to each blade's pitch horn via pitch links, all blades change pitch by the same amount at the same time, regardless of their position in the rotation. The collective also has a friction lock to hold position (so you don't have to grip it constantly) and a throttle twist grip on many types. Increasing collective increases blade angle of attack, which increases drag, which tends to slow the rotor—so you need more power (throttle) as you pull collective. On the R22, the throttle correlator automatically adjusts throttle with collective movement.
exam_tip: Collective: changes all blade pitch simultaneously. Up = more pitch = more lift = more drag = need more power. Connected to swashplate (moves it vertically). Has friction lock and often a twist-grip throttle. Throttle correlator adjusts power automatically.
key_facts: Collective: lever beside left seat. Changes pitch of all blades simultaneously (equally). Lever up = increased pitch = more lift = more drag on blades. Connected via pushrods/bellcranks to swashplate. Raising collective lifts entire swashplate vertically. All blades change pitch equally regardless of rotational position. Friction lock holds position. Twist-grip throttle on collective (many types). Increasing collective increases power required. Throttle correlator auto-adjusts power with collective.
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Describe the: — (a) purpose of the collective control
key_facts: [PENDING]

syllabus_desc: Describe the: — (b) principle of the operation of the collective control
key_facts: [PENDING]

syllabus_desc: Describe the purpose of a throttle cam-linkage (correlating unit).
concept: The throttle cam-linkage (also called a correlating unit or correlator) is a mechanical device that automatically adjusts engine throttle when you move the collective. Here's the problem it solves: when you raise the collective, blade pitch increases, which increases aerodynamic drag on the blades, which tends to slow the rotor RPM. You need more engine power to maintain RPM. Without a correlator, you'd have to manually twist the throttle every time you moved the collective—which is workable but adds workload, especially during manoeuvres where you're already busy. The correlator uses a cam (a shaped mechanical profile) linked between the collective and the engine throttle. As you raise the collective, the cam profile mechanically opens the throttle by an amount designed to roughly match the increased power demand. It's not perfect—it's a fixed mechanical relationship that can't account for every flight condition—so the pilot fine-tunes with the twist grip. But it takes care of the gross adjustment, letting you focus on flying. On the R22, the correlator is supplemented by a governor that fine-tunes RPM automatically. Between the two, RPM management becomes much more manageable.
exam_tip: Throttle cam-linkage/correlator: mechanical link between collective and throttle. Collective up = throttle opens automatically. Not perfect—pilot fine-tunes with twist grip. R22 has both correlator and governor.
key_facts: Throttle cam-linkage (correlator): mechanical link between collective and engine throttle. Collective up → cam opens throttle automatically. Compensates for increased drag from higher blade pitch. Fixed mechanical relationship—approximate correction only. Pilot fine-tunes RPM with twist-grip throttle. Reduces pilot workload during collective changes. R22: correlator + governor for RPM management. Governor provides fine-tuning beyond correlator's gross adjustment.
source: Wagtendonk Principles of Helicopter Flight, FAA Helicopter Handbook

syllabus_desc: Describe the purpose of the twist grip throttle.
concept: Even with a governor, you've got a twist grip throttle on the cyclic (or sometimes a separate lever) for fine RPM adjustment. It lets you trim the engine RPM slightly—useful for idle management at reduced power, managing engine temperature, or fine-tuning performance in high-altitude or hot conditions. You rotate it to increase RPM in the idle range or reduce it to trim power. It's a subtle control that requires finesse—coarse inputs on the twist grip cause power swings. The R22 and R44 both use twist-grip throttles, and learning smooth throttle feel is part of developing as a helicopter pilot.
exam_tip: Twist throttle is fine control, used for idle and RPM trim.
key_facts: Fine RPM adjustment control independent of collective
Manages idle power and engine temperature
Requires smooth, progressive inputs to avoid power surges
Common on R22 and R44 helicopters
source: Wagtendonk Principles of Helicopter Flight, FAA Helicopter Handbook

syllabus_desc: Describe the: (a) purpose of the cyclic control; (b) principle of operation of the cyclic control.
concept: The cyclic control is the stick between your knees (or in front of you). Its purpose is to tilt the main rotor disc in the direction you want to go—push forward, disc tilts forward, helicopter moves forward. Push right, disc tilts right, helicopter moves right. The principle: the cyclic connects through pushrods and bellcranks to the non-rotating lower swashplate. Moving the cyclic tilts the swashplate. As the rotating upper swashplate spins with the blades, each blade's pitch link rises and falls once per revolution following the tilted plate. This means each blade's pitch changes cyclically—more pitch on one side of the disc, less on the opposite side. More pitch means more lift on that side, which causes that blade to flap up (due to gyroscopic precession, this actually occurs 90 degrees later). The net effect is the rotor disc tilts in the direction you moved the cyclic. Because of the gyroscopic precession effect, the control inputs are actually applied about 90 degrees before where you want the effect—the mechanical linkage is designed to account for this phase angle.
exam_tip: Cyclic: tilts rotor disc in desired direction. Works by tilting swashplate → cyclic pitch variation → unequal lift → disc tilt. Gyroscopic precession: effect occurs 90° after force applied. Linkage accounts for this phase angle.
key_facts: Cyclic control: stick between pilot's knees. Tilts main rotor disc in desired direction. Connected to non-rotating swashplate via pushrods/bellcranks. Tilts swashplate → rotating plate varies blade pitch cyclically. Each blade: pitch changes once per revolution following tilted plate. More pitch on one side = more lift = blade flaps up. Gyroscopic precession: effect 90° after force application. Mechanical linkage compensates for 90° phase angle. Forward cyclic = forward disc tilt = forward flight.
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Describe the: — (a) purpose of the cyclic control
key_facts: [PENDING]

syllabus_desc: Describe the: — (b) principle of operation of the cyclic control
key_facts: [PENDING]

syllabus_desc: Describe the: (a) purpose of the tail rotor pedal controls; (b) principle of operation of the tail rotor pedal controls.
concept: The tail rotor pedals control the pitch of the tail rotor blades, giving you yaw control—the ability to point the helicopter's nose left or right. Left pedal increases tail rotor pitch (in most designs), producing more sideways thrust, which swings the nose left. Right pedal decreases pitch, reducing thrust, and torque reaction swings the nose right. In the hover, you're constantly making small pedal adjustments to maintain heading because power changes (collective movements) change the torque the tail rotor needs to counteract. More power = more torque = more tail rotor pitch needed (more left pedal on most types). The pedals connect to the tail rotor pitch change mechanism through cables or push-pull tubes running the length of the tail boom, ending at the pitch change spider or crosshead at the tail rotor hub. Unlike the cyclic and collective which work through the swashplate, the pedals directly change tail rotor blade pitch. In forward flight, the vertical fin provides a sideways aerodynamic force that partially replaces tail rotor thrust, so less pedal input is needed. At speeds above roughly 50-60 knots, the fin is doing most of the anti-torque work.
exam_tip: Pedals: control tail rotor pitch = yaw control. Left pedal = more pitch = more thrust = nose left (most types). Right pedal = less pitch = nose right. Power changes require pedal adjustment. Vertical fin offloads tail rotor in forward flight.
key_facts: Tail rotor pedals: control tail rotor blade pitch. Left pedal: increases pitch, more thrust, nose yaws left (most designs). Right pedal: decreases pitch, less thrust, nose yaws right. Connected via cables or push-pull tubes through tail boom. Controls pitch change spider/crosshead at tail rotor hub. Power changes require pedal adjustments (torque changes). More power = more torque = more left pedal needed. Vertical fin provides anti-torque in forward flight. Above ~50-60 knots: fin does most anti-torque work.
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the requirement for a dual purpose tachometer in a helicopter.
concept: A dual-purpose tachometer is required in a helicopter because the relationship between engine RPM and rotor RPM is the most critical parameter you monitor. In normal powered flight, the engine drives the rotor through the transmission at a fixed gear ratio, so both should read the same percentage (needles married). If they split apart, it means something's wrong in the drive train—a slipping clutch, a failing freewheeling unit, or an engine power loss. Without both readings on one instrument, you'd have to cross-reference two separate gauges, which is slower and more prone to error. The dual display lets you instantly see the relationship: married needles = normal, split = investigate immediately. During autorotation, the split is expected—engine RPM drops while rotor RPM is maintained by airflow. The tachometer confirms the freewheeling unit has disengaged properly. During start-up, it confirms the clutch is engaging progressively. In an emergency, a quick glance tells you whether the engine is still driving the rotor. It's the single most important instrument for drive train health monitoring in a helicopter.
exam_tip: Dual tachometer required because engine-rotor relationship is critical. Married needles = normal. Split = drive train issue (or autorotation). Faster to read one instrument than cross-reference two. Essential for: clutch engagement, freewheeling unit status, drive train health.
key_facts: Dual-purpose tachometer: displays engine RPM and rotor RPM on one instrument. Required because engine-rotor RPM relationship is critical monitoring parameter. Normal flight: needles married (same percentage). Split needles indicate: clutch slip, freewheeling unit disengagement, engine power loss. Autorotation: expected split (engine drops, rotor maintained). Start-up: confirms progressive clutch engagement. Single instrument faster than cross-referencing two gauges. Most important instrument for drive train health monitoring.
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Describe the indications on the engine/rotor tachometer during an autorotation: (a) with the engine running; (b) without the engine running.
concept: During autorotation, the engine/rotor tachometer shows distinct indications depending on conditions. With the engine operating (practice autorotation with throttle at flight idle): the engine needle drops to idle RPM while the rotor needle should be maintained in the green arc through collective management. The two needles split apart—rotor RPM above engine RPM, confirming the freewheeling unit has disengaged. With the engine stopped (actual engine failure): the engine needle drops to zero while rotor RPM must be maintained through energy management. In both cases, the pilot monitors rotor RPM to stay within limits—too low risks rotor stall, too high risks overspeed and structural damage.
exam_tip: Autorotation: needles split—rotor above engine. Engine running: engine at idle, rotor in green. Engine stopped: engine at zero. Monitor rotor RPM limits carefully.
key_facts: Engine running autorotation: engine at idle RPM
Rotor RPM maintained in green arc
Needles split: rotor above engine
Engine failure: engine needle drops to zero
Rotor RPM too low = stall, too high = overspeed
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, Principles of Helicopter Flight (Wagtendonk)

syllabus_desc: Explain the meaning of the following terms: (a) arm (moment arm); (b) datum; (c) moment (including units used); (d) centre of gravity (C of G); Advisory Circular AC61-5 Revision 38 Sub Topic Syllabus Item (e) longitudinal C of G range and limits; (f) lateral centre of gravity range and limits; (g) station; (h) index units; (i) basic empty weight (empty aircraft weight); (j) empty weight centre of gravity position; (k) basic operating weight (aircraft prepared for service weight); (l) payload (commercial load); (m) zero fuel weight (ZFW); (n) ramp weight; (o) gross weight (AUW); (p) take-off weight; (q) maximum certificated take-off weight (MCTOW); (r) landing weight; (s) maximum certificated landing weight (MCLW); (t) centre of gravity envelope; (u) the specific gravity and the weight of fuel of AVGAS or Jet A-1.
concept: Weight and balance is all about numbers and distance. The datum is an imaginary vertical reference line (usually at or near the nose of the aircraft) from which all distances are measured. A station is a measurement along the fuselage (or wing) from the datum—station 50 means 50 inches forward of datum, station 120 means 120 inches. The arm (or moment arm) is the horizontal distance from the datum to a particular load. A moment is weight multiplied by arm—it's a measure of turning effect around the datum. Moment units are typically pound-inches (lb-in) or kilogram-centimetres (kg-cm). Centre of gravity is the imaginary point where all the aircraft's weight is concentrated; if the aircraft hung from that point, it would balance perfectly. The C of G range (or envelope) is a zone within which the C of G must stay for safe flight—typically a 5-10% range of the mean aerodynamic chord. Basic empty weight is the aircraft as delivered from the factory, completely empty (no fuel, no people, no cargo). Empty weight C of G is where that empty aircraft's balance point is. Basic operating weight (or prepared for service weight) is the empty aircraft plus essential crew and equipment. Payload is all the useful load you can add—people, cargo. Zero fuel weight (ZFW) is the total weight without fuel. Ramp weight is total weight when ready to push back (with fuel). Gross weight or AUW (All Up Weight) is total weight ready for takeoff. MCTOW is the maximum weight at which the aircraft can legally take off—dictated by structural limits or performance limits. Landing weight is what you expect to weigh at touchdown (usually less than takeoff weight due to fuel burn). MCLW is the maximum weight at which you can legally land. Specific gravity is a ratio of the density of a substance to water (1.0). AVGAS has a specific gravity around 0.70-0.72, so it's lighter than water. Jet A-1 is around 0.80. These numbers matter because they let you convert volume to weight (litres × specific gravity × density of water = weight).
exam_tip: Memorise the definitions and the units. Moment = weight × arm (lb-in or kg-cm). C of G is where balance point is. Range is where C of G must stay.
key_facts: Datum is arbitrary zero reference point (usually aircraft nose)
Station is distance in inches (or cm) from datum along fuselage
Arm is distance from datum to centre of load or component
Moment is product of weight and arm (lb-in or kg-cm units)
Centre of gravity is balance point of all aircraft weight
C of G range (envelope) is safe zone for C of G location
Basic empty weight is aircraft structure only, no fuel/people/cargo
Empty weight C of G is balance point of empty aircraft
Basic operating weight is empty plus essential crew/equipment
Payload is all useful load that can be added (people, cargo)
Zero fuel weight (ZFW) is total weight without fuel
Ramp weight is total ready to push back (with fuel)
Gross weight (AUW) is total weight ready for takeoff
MCTOW is maximum legal takeoff weight (structural or performance limit)
Landing weight is expected weight at touchdown
MCLW is maximum legal landing weight
Specific gravity is density ratio (AVGAS ~0.71, Jet A-1 ~0.80)
Weight of fuel = volume × specific gravity × water density
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Given the appropriate conversion data: (a) calculate the weight of a given volume of fuel; (b) convert between US gallons and litres; (c) convert between pounds and kilograms.
concept: You've got to calculate everything. Takeoff weight: basic empty weight + crew + fuel + cargo. Landing weight: same minus burnt fuel. Fuel weight: litres × specific gravity (0.72 for AVGAS, ~0.8 for AVTUR). Convert US gallons to litres (1 US gal = 3.785 L). Convert pounds to kilograms (1 lb = 0.454 kg). Every weight goes into the calculation; nothing's negligible. Then you calculate each item's moment, add all moments, divide by total weight to find CG position in inches or percentage from datum. Plot it on the CG envelope—it's gotta fall within the green band. If CG's outside limits, reposition cargo or offload weight until it's good.
exam_tip: Practice calculations. Real exams and check-rides test your arithmetic and reasoning.
key_facts: Takeoff weight = empty weight + fuel + crew + cargo
Fuel weight = litres × 0.72 (AVGAS specific gravity)
US gallons × 3.785 = litres; pounds × 0.454 = kilograms
CG position = Σ moments ÷ total weight

syllabus_desc: Explain the effect of operating a helicopter outside of its weight limits.
concept: Exceed max takeoff weight and your helicopter suffers immediately. Reduced performance: longer takeoff, sluggish climb, lower hover ceiling. Structural stress: the airframe wasn't designed for that load—fatigue cracks develop. Longer approach speeds and shallower glide paths make landing difficult. You might not be able to clear obstacles during departure. Stability deteriorates. If you discover you're overweight after strapping in, you offload cargo or fuel until you're within limits—non-negotiable. The limits are there because engineers found the breaking point in testing and backed off a safety margin.
exam_tip: Overweight = degraded performance and structural risk. Calculate W&B before every flight.
key_facts: Exceeding max takeoff weight reduces climb performance
Structural loads exceed design limits, causing fatigue
Increased takeoff distance and approach speed
Reduced obstacle clearance and hover ceiling

syllabus_desc: From appropriate loading data/graphs for a typical light helicopter: (a) calculate the take-off weight; (b) calculate the landing weight; (c) calculate the landing weight for a limited take-off weight flight; (d) given a maximum take-off weight, calculate the payload available; (e) given a maximum zero fuel weight, calculate the payload available; (f) calculate the allowable load in various compartments.
concept: Using loading data and graphs for a typical light helicopter, you must be able to: calculate take-off weight from empty weight plus crew, passengers, fuel, and baggage; determine if the aircraft is within weight limits for the planned operation; calculate fuel required for the flight and determine useful load remaining after fuel; use manufacturer's loading graphs or tables to verify compliance with performance limitations. These calculations use the same arithmetic as aeroplane weight calculations but helicopter-specific factors include the effect of hovering performance on maximum take-off weight, which varies with density altitude and wind conditions.
exam_tip: Same weight calculation principles as aeroplanes. Add: empty weight + crew + passengers + fuel + baggage = take-off weight. Must be within limits. Hovering performance affects max weight.
key_facts: Calculate TOW from empty weight plus payload and fuel
Verify within maximum weight limits
Calculate fuel required and remaining useful load
Use manufacturer's loading data/graphs
Hovering performance affects max TOW
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, Principles of Helicopter Flight (Wagtendonk)

syllabus_desc: Explain the principles of helicopter balance.
concept: Helicopter balance works on the same principle as a seesaw—weight times distance from the pivot point (the fulcrum). In a helicopter, the 'fulcrum' is the main rotor mast. Everything forward of the mast creates a nose-down moment, everything aft creates a nose-up moment. The helicopter is balanced when these moments are equal, or more precisely, when the centre of gravity (CG) falls within the permitted range around the mast. Unlike fixed-wing aircraft where CG is measured along the longitudinal axis, helicopters are sensitive to both longitudinal (fore-aft) and lateral (left-right) CG position. Longitudinal CG affects how much cyclic input you need to maintain a level attitude—forward CG means more aft cyclic, aft CG means more forward cyclic. If CG is too far out of range, you may not have enough cyclic authority to control the aircraft. Lateral CG affects hover attitude—the helicopter will hang one skid low. The R22 with just two seats and a small baggage area has tight CG limits. Larger helicopters with external loads (underslung cargo) have to be especially careful about CG position.
exam_tip: Helicopter balance: weight × arm about main rotor mast. CG must be within limits both longitudinally and laterally. Forward CG = more aft cyclic needed. Aft CG = more forward cyclic. Outside limits = possible loss of cyclic authority.
key_facts: Balance principle: weight × arm (distance from reference point). Main rotor mast is the effective pivot point. Forward CG: nose-down tendency, needs more aft cyclic. Aft CG: nose-up tendency, needs more forward cyclic. Both longitudinal and lateral CG matter in helicopters. CG outside limits: may exceed cyclic authority. R22: tight CG limits due to small airframe. External loads (underslung cargo): significant CG considerations.
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Explain the effect of operating a helicopter outside of the centre of gravity limits.
concept: Operating outside CG limits is dangerous in a helicopter—more immediately dangerous than in most fixed-wing aircraft. Forward CG beyond limits: the helicopter requires excessive aft cyclic to maintain attitude. You may run out of aft cyclic authority, meaning you physically can't pull back enough to flare for landing or control a nose-down attitude. In forward flight, you'll struggle to slow down or stop. Aft CG beyond limits: you need excessive forward cyclic. You might not have enough forward cyclic to prevent a nose-up attitude, which can lead to loss of airspeed and a potential retreating blade stall situation. In the hover, aft CG can make the helicopter uncontrollable—it wants to pitch up and drift backward, and if you can't stop it, you end up in a rearward flight condition that's extremely dangerous. Lateral CG out of limits causes the helicopter to hang to one side, requiring constant cyclic correction. This reduces cyclic authority in that direction and can make the helicopter difficult to control in gusty conditions. In all cases, the fundamental problem is the same: you've reduced or eliminated your control margin.
exam_tip: Forward CG: may run out of aft cyclic (can't flare or slow down). Aft CG: may run out of forward cyclic (nose-up, rearward drift, retreating blade stall risk). Lateral: constant cyclic correction, reduced authority. All reduce control margins.
key_facts: Forward CG beyond limits: excessive aft cyclic needed. Risk: insufficient aft cyclic for flare or deceleration. Aft CG beyond limits: excessive forward cyclic needed. Risk: uncontrollable pitch-up, rearward drift, retreating blade stall. Lateral CG out of limits: constant cyclic correction, reduced authority. Fundamental problem: reduced or eliminated control margin. More immediately dangerous in helicopters than fixed-wing. Aft CG especially dangerous in hover.

syllabus_desc: Given loading data/graphs for a typical light helicopter: Advisory Circular AC61-5 Revision 38 Sub Topic Syllabus Item (a) calculate the longitudinal centre of gravity position; (b) calculate the lateral centre of gravity position; (c) plot the longitudinal and lateral centre of gravity positions on a graph showing the centre of gravity limits.
concept: Given loading data or graphs for a typical light helicopter, you must be able to calculate the centre of gravity position and verify it falls within the approved CG envelope. This involves: determining the moment for each item (weight × arm from datum), summing all moments, and dividing total moment by total weight to find the CG position. Then plot CG position against total weight on the CG envelope chart to confirm compliance. Helicopter CG calculations follow the same mathematical principles as aeroplane calculations, but the CG envelope is typically much smaller due to the sensitivity of rotor systems to CG displacement, particularly in the longitudinal axis.
exam_tip: CG = total moment / total weight. Plot CG vs weight on envelope chart. Helicopter CG envelope is smaller than aeroplane—more sensitive to loading.
key_facts: Moment = weight × arm from datum
CG position = total moment / total weight
Plot on CG envelope to verify compliance
Helicopter CG envelope typically smaller than aeroplane
Longitudinal CG particularly sensitive in helicopters
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38, Principles of Helicopter Flight (Wagtendonk)

key_facts: [PENDING]

syllabus_desc: Solve the following loading problems: — (b) loading or offloading weight to place the longitudinal centre of gravity at a given station
key_facts: [PENDING]

syllabus_desc: Solve the following loading problems: — (c) loading or offloading weight at a given station without exceeding the longitudinal centre of gravity limits

syllabus_desc: Solve the following loading problems: — (d) moving weight from one station to another and calculating the revised longitudinal centre of gravity position

syllabus_desc: Explain the likely effect of a displaced lateral centre of gravity on the position of the cyclic control.
concept: As you fly, you burn fuel. If you've got more fuel in the aft tank, burning it shifts CG forward (removes aft weight). If forward tank has more, burning it shifts CG aft (removes forward weight). Smart loading manages this: you deliberately load more fuel aft initially so that as you cruise and burn fuel, CG drifts forward but stays in limits. On a long flight, this is critical planning. Some helicopters have automated fuel transfer systems; you select 'cruise' and the system keeps CG constant by moving fuel between tanks as you fly. Manual helicopters require you to manage crossfeed.
exam_tip: Understand fuel burn shifts CG. Plan loading so CG stays in envelope throughout flight.
key_facts: Burning aft-tank fuel moves CG forward
Burning forward-tank fuel moves CG aft
Load more fuel aft initially to manage CG drift during cruise
Use crossfeed or fuel transfer systems to maintain CG in limits

syllabus_desc: Explain the effect of external loads on the centre of gravity position.
concept: Adding or removing weight shifts CG. Add weight forward of current CG, CG moves forward. Add weight aft, CG moves aft. This is intuitive. The further the weight from the current CG, the bigger the shift. Loading large fuel quantities in the aft tank shifts CG back significantly. You plan your loading order: fuel to the forward tank first, then aft, so CG stays in limits as you load. If you add cargo, you calculate the new CG and confirm it's still good. Offloading reverses the effect—drop cargo from the back, CG moves forward.
exam_tip: Understand the direction of CG shift with loading changes.
key_facts: Loading forward of current CG moves CG forward
Loading aft of current CG moves CG aft
Greater distance = greater CG shift
Fuel loading order affects CG progression during fuel load

syllabus_desc: Solve the following loading problems: (a) find a revised centre of gravity position after loading or offloading weight; (b) loading or offloading weight to place the longitudinal centre of gravity at a given station; (c) loading or offloading weight at a given station without exceeding the centre of gravity limits; (d) moving weight from one station to another and finding the new centre of gravity position.
concept: Loading problems in helicopter weight and balance follow the same maths as fixed-wing. To find revised CG after adding or removing weight: calculate the new total moment (old total moment ± added/removed weight × its arm), then divide by the new total weight. That gives you the new CG position. To place CG at a specific station: you need to work out what weight at what arm will shift the CG to your target. The formula is: required weight = total weight × (desired CG – current CG) ÷ (arm of added weight – desired CG). For loading weight without exceeding CG limits: determine the maximum weight you can add at a given station that keeps CG within the envelope. Calculate both the forward and aft CG limits and find how much weight at the given arm keeps you within both. Moving weight from one station to another: the CG shift = (weight moved × distance moved) ÷ total weight. This gives you the change in CG position. These problems are straightforward arithmetic once you set them up properly. Draw a diagram, label your arms and weights, work through the moments table methodically. Don't rush the maths—a calculation error here could put you outside CG limits on a real flight.
exam_tip: Key formulas: New CG = new total moment ÷ new total weight. CG shift from moving weight = (weight × distance moved) ÷ total weight. Set up a moments table every time. Draw diagrams. Check your answer makes sense (CG should move toward where you add weight).
key_facts: Revised CG: new total moment ÷ new total weight. New total moment = old moment ± (added/removed weight × arm). Weight to achieve target CG: total weight × (desired CG – current CG) ÷ (arm – desired CG). CG shift from moving weight: (weight moved × distance moved) ÷ total weight. Loading within limits: check both forward and aft CG limits. Set up moments table for every calculation. CG moves toward point where weight is added.

syllabus_desc: Explain the likely effect of fuel burn on the centre of gravity position.
concept: As you burn fuel in flight, the helicopter's weight decreases—but the CG also shifts, and which direction depends on where the fuel tank is relative to the current CG. If the fuel tank is aft of the CG (common in many light helicopters), burning fuel removes weight from behind the CG, so the CG moves forward. If the tank is forward of the CG, burning fuel shifts CG aft. The R22's fuel tank is located under and slightly behind the seats, which means as fuel burns off, CG tends to shift forward. This is generally the safer direction—better to end a flight with forward CG (where you have good aft cyclic authority for the flare) than aft CG. You need to plan for both the takeoff CG (full fuel, heavy) and the landing CG (fuel burned, lighter). Both must be within limits. On a long flight, the CG might start near one limit and migrate toward the other. Helicopter flight manuals include fuel burn CG charts or tables for this reason. A common exam scenario: you take off within limits with full fuel and two heavy passengers, but after burning fuel, the aft passenger weight dominates and CG shifts outside the aft limit.
exam_tip: Fuel burn shifts CG: tank aft of CG → fuel burn moves CG forward. Tank forward of CG → fuel burn moves CG aft. Check both takeoff and landing CG. R22: fuel tank behind/below seats, CG moves forward as fuel burns.
key_facts: Fuel burn decreases weight and shifts CG. Direction depends on fuel tank position relative to CG. Tank aft of CG: fuel burn moves CG forward. Tank forward of CG: fuel burn moves CG aft. R22: fuel tank aft of CG, CG moves forward with fuel burn. Must check both takeoff CG and landing CG within limits. CG can migrate from one limit toward another during flight. Flight manual provides fuel burn CG charts/tables.
